Other Pathway Electives — Kaplan, Esports, AWS, Study Away, Transfer

Kaplan Institute / Startup (3)

  • Startup Studio Event Series (6–10 hr)
  • Chicago Innovation Ecosystem Explorer (8–15 hr) — 1871, mHUB, Founders Institute, TechRise, WBDC, Matter, UTP, Future Founders
  • IPRO Innovation Day Exploration (2–3 hr)

Esports & Digital Arts (4)

  • Gaming Content Creator Business Dev (15–25 hr)
  • Esports Event Production & Management (12–20 hr)
  • Game Design & Development Foundations (10–15 hr) — Everyone Can Code Chicago
  • Esports Analytics Research (15–25 hr) — 1,211+ Discord community

AWS Academy & Certifications (7)

  • AWS Cloud Foundations (20 hr, free + 50% exam discount)
  • AWS Cloud Developing (40 hr)
  • AWS Cloud Architecting (40 hr)
  • AWS Machine Learning Foundations (20 hr)
  • AWS Security Specialty (40–60 hr)
  • Salesforce Foundations / Camp4 Consulting (15–25 hr)
  • Global Career Accelerator / Podium (3 credits, ~45+ hr)

Study Away & Global (6)

  • Semester Exchange (1 sem) — partner universities worldwide
  • Summer Study Abroad (4–8 wk)
  • Faculty-Led Short-Term (1–3 wk)
  • Research Abroad (4–12 wk)
  • Service Learning Abroad (1–4 wk)
  • Globe Trotter DIY International (1–2 wk min, student-selected)

Transfer Student Pilot (4)

  • Transfer Orientation & Onboarding (2–3 hr)
  • Credit & Experience Mapping (3–5 hr) — prior-learning audit
  • Transfer Peer Mentorship (10+ hr, semester-long)
  • Accelerated Badge Portfolio (5–10 hr async) — retroactive badges

How Tracks & Checklists Work

The Elevate program organizes activities into Tracks (year-based pathways). Each track has a Primary Checklist with required and elective activities, plus Self-Support Checklists for orientation, resources, and optional development.

Year 1: Exposure

First-Year Professional Development

  • 1 Primary Checklist (23 activities)
  • 6 Required + 17 Elective options
  • + Self-support checklists for resources
  • 276 students enrolled

Year 2: Engagement

Sophomore Professional Development

  • 1 Primary Checklist (22 activities)
  • 7 Required + 15 Elective options
  • + Self-support checklists for resources
  • 120 students enrolled

Year 1 Checklist: Required & Elective Activities (23 Items)

First-Year Elevate Success Coach Check-in
REQUIRED
30-minute meeting to review college transition, academic success strategies, and co-curricular planning. Students who complete check-ins are 3x more likely to maintain good standing.
30 minYear 1 Check-In, Goal Setting
Resume Development with Career Services
REQUIRED
Design your first collegiate resume using Illinois Tech template. Students with reviewed resumes receive 3x more interview invitations.
1-2 hrsJob Search Portfolio
Elevate Mentorship Programs
REQUIRED
Apply for Illinois Tech First-Year Mentorship or approved external program. Meet at least twice per semester.
OngoingProfessional Relationship Builder
Find Your Flock - Student Org Membership
REQUIRED
Join and actively participate in at least ONE student organization. Attend minimum 3 meetings or events. 150+ organizations available.
VariesCollaboration Champion, Community
First-Year Elevate Experience (3 Hours)
REQUIRED
Complete 3 total contact hours of approved professional exposure experiences: career fairs, info sessions, job shadowing, workshops.
3 hrs totalIndustry Navigator
Chicago Adventures - Community Engagement
REQUIRED
Explore Chicago's 77 neighborhoods through cultural experiences, museums (free for students!), or community events. Submit 300-word reflection.
VariesGlobal Citizen, Community Impact
IPRO Innovation Day Exploration
ELECTIVE
Attend Innovation Day, engage with 3 interdisciplinary projects, submit 400-word reflection on teamwork and collaboration.
2-3 hrsCritical Thinker, Innovation Catalyst
ELM Leadership Program
ELECTIVE
Empowerment, Leadership & Mentorship program with 3-tier badge system. Develop transferable leadership skills employers demand.
3-15+ hrsELM Practitioner/Specialist/Mentor
Community Connections - Service Day
ELECTIVE
Volunteer in Bronzeville neighborhood. Serve your community while building professional skills and connections.
3-4 hrsCommunity Impact Leader
LinkedIn Profile Creation
ELECTIVE
Build All-Star LinkedIn profile. Profiles with photos receive 21x more views. Connect with 20+ people.
1-2 hrsDigital Literacy L1
Career Fair Prep Squad - Undergrad Hour
ELECTIVE
Special morning session to practice with real recruiters before the career fair. Low pressure, high reward!
1 hrProfessional Communications
Hawks in Action - Athletics
ELECTIVE
Join varsity, club, or intramural sports. 94% of Fortune 500 CEOs played college sports!
40+ hrsCollaboration, Leadership, Time Mgmt
Hawk Ambassador Program
ELECTIVE
Lead campus tours and recruit future Hawks. Develop public speaking and leadership skills.
20+ hrsProfessional Communications, Leadership
AWS Cloud Skills & Certifications
ELECTIVE
FREE access to 17 AWS programs. Cloud skills = 25% higher starting salaries! Contact Dr. Freddrick Logan.
10-40 hrsDigital Literacy L3+, Technology Pioneer
Globe Trotter - International Cultural Immersion
ELECTIVE
1+ weeks abroad exploring culture. Study abroad, family trips, or solo adventures. Complete cultural challenges.
40+ hrsGlobal Citizen, Cultural Competency
Hope Chicago & Chicago Difference
ELECTIVE
Special support programs for Chicago-area students. Additional financial support and mentorship.
2-5 hrs/monthCommunity Champion
Elevator Pitch - Wild Card Experience
ELECTIVE
Turn ANY professional experience into Elevate credit. Pitch your unique opportunity for approval.
10+ hrsCustom badge alignment
Flight Plan - Second Custom Experience
ELECTIVE
Design your second wild card experience to fill skill gaps. Stack experiences for multiple competencies.
10+ hrsCustom badge alignment

Year 2 Checklist: Required & Elective Activities (22 Items)

Sophomore Elevate Experience (10+ Hours)
REQUIRED
Create professional work product: Forage simulation, certification, hackathon project, or research. 87% of completers land Y3 internships.
10+ hrsPortfolio Builder, Professional Experience
Handshake Profile Optimization
REQUIRED
Complete and optimize to 90%+. Students with complete profiles receive 5x more internship interviews.
1-2 hrsJob Search Ready, Digital Literacy
Sophomore Elevate Success Coach Check-In
REQUIRED
30-minute meeting focused on internship strategies and career planning. 3x more likely to secure internships.
30 minCareer Planning, Internship Ready
LinkedIn Profile (Required Y2)
REQUIRED
Build professional brand. 93% of recruiters use LinkedIn. Complete profiles receive 40x more opportunities.
1-2 hrsProfessional Brand, Networking
Resume Review (Y2 Required)
REQUIRED
Get internship-ready resume reviewed. Students with reviewed resumes receive 2x more interview callbacks.
1-2 hrsResume Ready, Job Search
Professional Development Workshop
REQUIRED
Attend "Sophomore Approved" workshop on interview skills, salary negotiation, personal branding, etc.
1-2 hrsProfessional Development
Leadership Academy (6 Workshops)
REQUIRED
Complete 6 leadership workshops. Leadership is #1 skill employers seek. 30% higher starting salaries!
6-12 hrsLeadership Ready, Team Collaboration
Student Organization Leadership
ELECTIVE
Hold leadership position for one semester. 82% of recruiters say this is "very important" in hiring.
3-5 hrs/weekCampus Leader, Team Management
Industry Research Deep Dive
ELECTIVE
Research 3 target companies using Galvin Library databases. Create 2-page intelligence reports. 3x more job offers!
8-10 hrsIndustry Intelligence, Research Pro
Mock Interview Session
ELECTIVE
60-minute practice interview with personalized feedback. 3x more likely to receive internship offers.
1 hr + prepInterview Ready, Professional Communications
Industry Networking Event
ELECTIVE
Attend career fair, industry panel, or employer info session. Connect with 3+ employers. 5x more internship offers.
2-4 hrsNetworking Pro, Career Explorer
Informational Interviews (2)
ELECTIVE
Complete 2 informational interviews with professionals. 70% of jobs are never posted - access hidden market!
2-4 hrsNetworking Skills, Career Explorer
Professional Portfolio Development
ELECTIVE
Create online portfolio with 3-5 projects. GitHub, Behance, or personal website. Makes employers say "WOW!"
4-8 hrsPortfolio Builder, Digital Literacy

School & College Pilots — Status, Next Steps, Expansion

Every school-level partnership at Illinois Tech with its current pilot status, immediate next steps, and 12–24 month expansion path.

PilotCurrent StatusNext Steps (Summer–Fall 2026)Expansion Path
Stuart School of BusinessACTIVE · 18 activities live on 312, C-Suite Pathway (CEO/COO/CFO/CTO/CMO) deployed, Stuart Career Edge course developingFinalize CMC data-sharing protocol; identify 1–2 Stuart courses for Fall 2026 curricular integration pilot; launch Career Edge course (Kurt)University-wide promotion of Stuart certificates; cross-school C-Suite (Future CTO with Computing); SIE/CAPM auto-earn scaling
Armour College of EngineeringBUILDING · Mentorship interest confirmed Apr 8 2026; 62% of Y1 co-curricular plan participants are engineeringFollow-up meeting May 28 (1–2 PM) with Melanie Jones + Michael Arnaudo; IPRO Innovation Day alignment; research-opportunities newsletter inclusionDedicated engineering badge pathways; AWS Academy + Quantum Computing integration; IPRO badge auto-trigger
College of ComputingBUILDING · 7 scoping questions answered; video call proposed with Matt Bauer; Tech+ project pathway in scopeTech+ Integration Specialist badge alignment; Future CTO C-Suite cross-school requirement finalization; coordinate with Stuart on Tech+ degreesComputing-specific badge tracks; hackathon auto-credit; quantum computing partnership
Architecture CollegeBUILDING · Initial follow-up sent to Kathleen Nagle + Mike Glynn; activity ideation in progressConfirm 5 activity areas (portfolio development workshops, design comp participation, firm site visits, interdisciplinary studio, licensure prep); pilot launch scopingPortfolio-focused badge progression; AXP / ARE licensure tracking; firm partnership pipeline
Lewis College (Humanities)EMERGING · Career Pivoting workshop developed with Emily; interdisciplinary student interest documentedCareer Pivoting + Transferable Skills workshop launch; cross-disciplinary case studies (physics→finance, architecture→UX); BUS 495 pathway promotionLiberal-arts-to-tech pivot framework; transferable skills badge; partnership with Lewis advisors
Pre-Health PartnershipPROPOSED · Dashboard page created, activity catalog scoping in progressConfirm 3 activity pillars (clinical exposure, research, grad-school readiness); engage Pre-Health advising; design culminating badges (Future MD/DO/PA)Stuart C-Suite-style stacked badges; MCAT prep integration; hospital partnership pipeline
Kaplan Institute / Startup StudioACTIVE · IPRO Innovation Day live; Chicago Innovation Ecosystem activity active; Startup Studio events ongoingCredit-bearing pathway exploration (Edo); Faculty Summer Camp participation; Smart Lab AWS Wed office hours rhythmEntrepreneurship credit-bearing track; Chicago innovation ecosystem partnership expansion (1871, mHUB, Founders Institute)
Braven PartnershipACTIVE · MOU signed; BRVN 200 Fall 2026 (3-credit hybrid); 2 sections planned; flyers + FAQ live; tabling May 4–9Launch BRVN 200 Fall 2026 in 2 sections (Wed + Thu); confirm Elevate Y2 experience-option credit; Cynthia Stewart approval threadingBraven Y2-experience-option scale to all eligible students; alumni mentor pipeline; employer-simulation integration
AWS Academy — Center of ExcellenceACTIVE · 5 academic schools, 17 cert pathways, 60–70+ students enrolled, free vouchers; AWS office visit Apr 29Launch summer 4-week cohorts (May 18/20 start); fall 8-week cadence; 100/cohort target; Wednesday Smart Lab office hoursUniversity-wide rolling cohorts; advanced certs (Cloud Architect, ML, Security Specialty); national-replication packaging
Athletics — Hawks in ActionACTIVE · Hawks in Action live; Hawks Beyond the Field elective; mentorship pipeline interest expressedExpand catalog beyond current 2 activities; design athlete-to-professional mentorship pathway; team captain leadership creditAthletics leadership track; alumni-athlete mentorship; NCAA compliance integration
SGA — Student Government AssociationACTIVE · Full Elevate walkthrough completed with Delwin Manjali + Yailine Cano; Charlie Janisch engagedCollaborative fall events; SGA-championed Elevate promotion; structural feedback loop on annual surveysSGA leadership auto-credit; student-staff badge pipeline; campus-wide ambassador network
Center for Learning Innovation (CLI)EXPLORATORY · CLI absorbed OPCE; uses Credly (over limits); just launched Ed2Go non-credit revenue-shareLauren Woods badge architecture handoff; Ed2Go-Elevate integration; faculty/staff badging through 312 setup; Faculty Summer CampUniversity-wide badge ecosystem; transcript integration with Registrar; AI badge recommender; alumni badge network
Esports & Digital ArtsEMERGING · 4 activities scoped (Gaming Content, Esports Event Production, Game Design, Esports Analytics)Discord community engagement (1,211+); Twitch/YouTube/TikTok creator pipeline; analytics research with Data Science partnershipDedicated badge tracks; industry partnerships; collegiate esports league integration

Featured · Interactive Explorers

Click, toggle, slide — explore the May 2026 numbers yourself

Three live, in-browser explorers. Click a tier in the badge architecture chart to filter the catalog. Toggle between All / Y1 / Y2 in the status comparator. Slide the month forward to see Elevate’s pace toward the Fall 2026 enrollment target. All data sourced from the May 11 2026 admin export and the May 2026 Student Activity Tracker.

Interactive Badge Architecture Explorer Click a slice

Total catalog: 73 badges across 3 architectural tiers. Click a doughnut slice or a tier card to drill into that tier’s badge list.

All 73 badges
Click a slice or a tier card above to drill in. The catalog is anchored to the May 10 2026 Master Reference Document and reconciled against the May 11 2026 admin export (1,074 cumulative badge instances; 137 unique earners).

Interactive Cohort & Status Comparator Toggle cohort

Status breakdown sourced from the May 12 2026 Student Activity Tracker (n=398, 290 Y1 + 108 Y2). The bar chart stacks Year 1 (scarlet) on Year 2 (teal) when viewing All, and shows a single cohort when filtered. All three statuses represent active program participation — once a student signs T&C they are In Progress. Students advance through In Progress → Close to Finished → Complete as they work the required activity ladder for their program year.

Strategic Priorities for Spring 2026

Priority 01
Resume Development Push
Only 4% completion—one of our weakest metrics. Implement mandatory workshops for Year 1 and require resume review at every Elevate Success Coach meeting.
Priority 02
Commuter Student Support
19% no-show rate indicates scheduling barriers. Launch virtual check-in options and strengthen outreach for commuter students through flexible scheduling.
Priority 03
Collaborative Events Expansion
Partner with academic departments, student organizations, employers, and university offices for Elevate-centric programming: Engineering career panels, Architecture firm tours, Business mixers.

Terms & Conditions

Official Elevate program requirements, eligibility criteria, and year-by-year expectations

Illinois Institute of Technology · Elevate Initiative

Elevate Terms & Conditions

Requirements Document · Career Services & Experiential Learning

Effective Date: September 15, 2025

1. ELEVATE DESCRIPTION

INTRODUCTION

Elevate is at the core of Illinois Tech's founding purpose and mission: Illinois Tech was founded in 1890 as an engine of opportunity so that children of meatpackers and machinists in Chicago could receive an excellent education and become engineers, architects, physicists, and more. Learning by doing things has always been a vital part of that mission: It's not only through books and lectures that students learn, but also through co-curricular hands-on experiences and practical applications. In today's world driven by globalization and dominated by multinational corporations, career readiness is critical to expand accessibility in modern economy.

PURPOSE

Elevate is designed to support students' academic and career growth using a data model with extensive analysis of student success variables and more than a decade of assessment of the student journey. The goal with Elevate is to help students gain experiences that will enhance their resumes throughout their college careers, and to provide skills fostering career-readiness for success in an evolving world.

ELEVATE

Illinois Tech's Elevate is designed to enhance your Illinois Tech experience and to support you throughout your learner lifecycle at Illinois Tech, starting from pre-enrollment, extending through post-enrollment and, if applicable, beyond post-graduation. Elevate combines hands-on experiential opportunities with a customized experiential learning and professional development plan (the "Elevate My Future Plan") developed in coordination with a team of career and alumni advisers, enhancing a graduate's career readiness and life skills.

CORE COMPONENTS OF ELEVATE:

  • Access to Elevate Platform: Access to the Elevate platform will be provided to students who acknowledge the requirements of the Elevate experience. Illinois Tech's Careers & Elevate Hub provides curated co-curricular and extracurricular activities and opportunities that encourage registered students to participate and advance their personal and professional development.
  • Elevate My Future Plan: Students participating in Elevate will work with advisers, success coaches, and peer mentors during your first year to create a personalized plan, mapping out a co-curricular activity and career pathway through the Elevate Platform, and an academic plan through Smart Plan.
  • Elevate Experiences: Elevate combines hands-on experiential opportunities, including short courses in general business topics, courses that introduce twenty-first century skills, student leadership opportunities, on-campus employment opportunities, competitions, study away virtual internships, internships, co-operative education and research with customized learning/experience plans.

If a student successfully completes all Elevate requirements, and an employment or graduate school offer is not achieved within six months of graduation, subject to additional factors as determined by Illinois Tech, the student may be eligible to enroll tuition-free in select graduate-level courses at Illinois Tech, up to a maximum of 9 credit hours, or by participating cost-free in relevant up-skilling programs or digital apprenticeships at Illinois Tech for the duration of one academic term. This is only available to students within one year of their graduation date.

2. ELIGIBILITY AND PARTICIPATION REQUIREMENTS

ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS FOR INITIAL ELEVATE PARTICIPATION:

  • Acknowledge the requirements of the Elevate experience. Only eligible students who execute the Elevate Acknowledgement in year one of their Illinois Tech enrollment may participate.
  • Actively participate in Elevate Orientation.
  • Regularly review, look for new opportunities, participate and accomplish the tasks outlined in the Elevate platform.
  • Opt-in and track participation, progress through the plan by completing required activities and earn Elevate experience badges for active participation or completion as applicable.
  • Meet with each member of Student Success team at least once a year for academic, financial and career plan reviews.
  • Complete a financial literacy workshop and create a financial plan to support your degree plan.
  • Remain in good financial standing throughout the degree program at Illinois Tech.
  • Maintain good academic standing and a 3.0 GPA and above throughout undergraduate degree program.
  • Complete all minimum expected requirements, and two optional experiences required during each year.
  • Graduate with a bachelor's degree within eight to 10 (for certain programs) semesters of enrollment.
  • Be in good financial standing with the university at the time of graduation.
  • Be legally eligible for employment in the United States upon graduation.
  • Be willing to relocate to a new market location, if necessary for employment, upon graduation.
  • Complete a pre-professional co-op and internship prep course.

YEAR-BY-YEAR GENERAL REQUIREMENTS TO MAINTAIN ELEVATE PARTICIPATION:

YEAR 1 · EXPOSURE

  • 1:1 check-in with career coach, cohort manager, or success coach and initiate co-curricular plan
  • Meet with Career Services to design, review, and complete 1st collegiate resume
  • Successfully complete an Elevate experience(s) that meets the following minimum expected requirements:
    • Aligns with field of study and/or future career path of interest
    • At least 3 hours of engagement
    • Proof of attendance is required
    • Synchronous (live in real-time, not recorded)
    • Co-curricular (not a part of a course requirement)
  • Apply for and participate in university peer mentorship program or external mentorship program
  • Chicago Adventures - Premiere City and Cultural Experiences (Community Engagement)
  • Student Organization Membership

YEAR 2 · ENGAGEMENT

  • Successfully complete an Elevate experience(s) that meets the following minimum expected requirements:
    • Aligns with field of study and/or future career path of interest
    • At least 10 hours of engagement
    • Must complete an activity where something tangible is produced
    • Format flexibility - can be in-person or virtual
    • Synchronous or Asynchronous (if experience is a job simulation or short course)
    • Co-curricular - must be an experience outside of the classroom
    • Requires proof of completion
  • Attend a second-year professional development workshop hosted by Career Services
  • 1:1 check-in with career coach, cohort manager, or success coach
  • Complete and optimize Handshake profile
  • Complete and optimize LinkedIn profile
  • Meet with Career Services to design, review, and complete 1st collegiate resume
  • Leadership Academy OR Approved Leadership Workshop Series

YEAR 3 · ACCOUNTABILITY

  • Successfully complete an Elevate experience(s) that meets the following minimum expected requirements:
    • Aligns with field of study and/or future career path of interest
    • At least 40 hours of engagement
    • Experience requires a supervisor or facilitator providing direction
    • Must be synchronous (live in real-time)
    • Co-curricular - must be an experience outside of the classroom
    • Provide proof of experience
  • Attending a professional development workshop hosted by Career Services or external conference
  • 1:1 check-in with career coach, cohort manager, or success coach

YEAR 4 · UPSKILLING

  • Successfully complete an Elevate experience(s) that meets the following minimum expected requirements:
    • Aligns with field of study and/or future career path of interest
    • At least 40+ hours of engagement
    • Can be a continuation of 3rd year experience or new upskilling experience
    • Co-curricular - must be an experience outside of the classroom
    • Provide proof of experience
  • Start and document job search or graduate school search at least six months in advance of graduation
  • 1:1 check-in with career coach, cohort manager, or success coach
  • Must apply (8) job applications per month and provide evidence – Handshake, Simplicity, LinkedIn – for external applications provide proof of completed application (i.e. email receipt)

YEAR 5 (IF NECESSARY) · UPSKILLING 2.0

  • Successfully complete an Elevate experience(s) that meets the following minimum expected requirements:
    • Aligns with field of study and/or future career path of interest
    • At least 40+ hours of engagement
    • Can be a continuation of 3rd year experience or new upskilling experience
    • Co-curricular - must be an experience outside of the classroom
    • Provide proof of experience
  • Start and document job search or graduate school search at least six months in advance of graduation
  • 1:1 check-in with career coach, cohort manager, or success coach
  • Must apply (8) job applications per month and provide evidence

YEAR-BY-YEAR ELECTIVE OPTIONS

MUST COMPLETE AT LEAST TWO FOR EACH YEAR

YEAR ELECTIVE OPTIONS (CHOOSE AT LEAST 2)
Year 1 • Complete Handshake profile
• Apply for on- or off-campus positions or provide proof of current off-campus job
• Participate in and successfully complete First-Year Experience program and General Learning Strategies class (if assigned)
• Have demonstrated participation and regular involvement in at least one significant campus co-curricular activity or events including, but not limited to, Hawk House, a professional student organization, and others
• Attend a first-year Professional Development workshop hosted by Career Services
Year 2 • Schedule and complete mock interview with feedback from Career Services
• Complete two leadership seminars and/or retreat through the leadership academy
• Maintain participation in peer mentor program (if started in year one)
• Attend a second-year Professional Development workshop hosted by Career Services
• Complete 21st Century Leadership Skills course online through Academic Impressions
• Complete General Learning Studies course (if assigned) and academic coaching
Year 3 • Meet with Academic Affairs for a degree audit check and possible consideration for Accelerated Master's Program
• Attend on-campus or virtual recruitment event (career fair, company information session, or similar)
• Complete additional ELM training and/or serve as a peer mentor for 1st year students
• Attend a professional development workshop hosted by Career Services
• Apply for and participate in university Alumni-Student Mentorship program
• Serve as a board member in a student organization or serve with the Student Government Association or lead a project/event as part of a student organization
Year 4 • Demonstrate attendance at minimum of two on-campus or virtual recruitment events (career fairs, company information sessions, or similar)
• Serve as a mentor in the university's peer mentorship program
• Attend a professional development workshop hosted by Career Services
• Participate in university Alumni-Student Mentorship program
• Serve as a board member in a student organization or serve with the Student Government Association
Year 5 • Demonstrate attendance at minimum of two on-campus or virtual recruitment events (career fairs, company information sessions, or similar)
• Serve as a mentor in the university's peer mentorship program
• Attend a professional development workshop hosted by Career Services
• Participate in university Alumni-Student Mentorship program
• Serve as a board member in a student organization or serve with the Student Government Association

References

Framework sources, research foundations, and relevant literature supporting the Elevate Program design

Career Readiness Frameworks

Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning. (2024). CASEL’s SEL framework: What are the core competence areas and where are they promoted? CASEL. https://casel.org/sel-framework/

National Association of Colleges and Employers. (2024). Career readiness: Competencies for a career-ready workforce. NACE. https://www.naceweb.org/career-readiness/competencies/career-readiness-defined/

Partnership for 21st Century Learning. (2019). Framework for 21st century learning. Battelle for Kids. https://www.battelleforkids.org/networks/p21

World Economic Forum. (2025). Future of jobs report 2025. World Economic Forum. https://www.weforum.org/publications/the-future-of-jobs-report-2025/

National Association of Colleges and Employers. (2026). Job outlook 2026 spring update (n=185). NACE. https://www.naceweb.org/

Coursera. (2024). Job skills report 2024. Coursera. https://www.coursera.org/

Coursera. (2025). Job skills report 2025. Coursera. https://www.coursera.org/

Coursera. (2026). Job skills report 2026. Coursera. https://www.coursera.org/

National Student Clearinghouse Research Center. (2024, January). Some College, No Credential student outcomes: Annual progress report — Academic Year 2022/23. National Student Clearinghouse. https://nscresearchcenter.org/some-college-no-credential/

Source for the 37.6M+ U.S. / 1.1M Illinois “Some College, No Credential” (SCNC) population cited in the Strategic Positioning hero (Learner Population II), Platform & Technology Roadmap stat banner, and the Expanding Access framework card. SCNC is the National Student Clearinghouse’s designation for adults who enrolled in college but never earned a credential — a distinct population from the broader group of Americans without a college degree.

Integrated Institutional Frameworks (APA citations)

These 12 frameworks are already integrated into Elevate’s Crosswalk, Workshop Series, badge architecture, and Transfer Bridge. The detail cards beneath the APA list describe the integration point for each.

University of Cincinnati. (2024). Cooperative education program (1906–present). University of Cincinnati. https://www.uc.edu/campus-life/career-education/co-op.html

Integrated Framework References (May 2026)

The 12 frameworks below are already integrated into Elevate’s design — they shape the Crosswalk, the Workshop Series, the badge architecture, the Workforce + Academic Hybrid Platform vision, and the Transfer Cohort Bridge. Each card names the source institution, the construct credentialed, and the integration point in the Elevate catalog so the citation trail is auditable for accreditation, employer partnerships, and senior-leadership briefings.

Harvard GSE — Pathways to Prosperity

National framework advocating structured pathways from secondary education through postsecondary into careers. Aligns with Elevate’s pre-college through employment-guarantee pipeline.

HBS — Managing the Future of Work

Research initiative on how technology, demographics, and globalization reshape work. Evidence base for skills-based hiring, experiential credentialing, and employer engagement.

MIT Experiential Learning Office (ELO)

MIT’s centralized model for coordinating undergraduate research, internships, community engagement, and global experiences. Informs Elevate’s multi-pathway badge progression.

MIT Teaching + Learning Lab — Digital Credentialing

Pioneer in digital credentialing and open badges. Foundation for Elevate’s digital-badge architecture, portable credentials, and Open Badges 3.0 roadmap.

Oxford Careers — Skills-Based Career Development

Competency framework mapping transferable skills across academic disciplines to career outcomes. Mirrors Elevate’s co-curricular integration strategy.

NACE Career Readiness Competencies

Eight industry-standard competencies: Career & Self-Development, Communication, Critical Thinking, Equity & Inclusion, Leadership, Professionalism, Teamwork, Technology. Core reference for Elevate’s 5-track badge system.

AAC&U High-Impact Practices (HIPs)

First-year seminars, learning communities, writing-intensive courses, collaborative assignments, undergraduate research, diversity/global learning, internships, capstones. Multiple HIPs embedded across Elevate.

Northeastern — Co-op & Experiential Learning

100+ years of practice alternating academic semesters with full-time work. Evidence base for Elevate’s employment-guarantee model and structured work-integrated learning.

Purdue-Gallup Index

Longitudinal study connecting experiential learning to long-term career outcomes and workplace engagement. Validates Elevate’s mentorship, applied projects, and engagement model.

Cincinnati — Cooperative Education

Birthplace of cooperative education (1906). Institutional-scale benchmark for Elevate’s program-wide approach.

Georgia Tech — Experiential Education Model

STEM-focused integration of undergraduate research, internships, and co-op. Mirrors Elevate’s AWS Academy integration and Armour engineering partnerships.

Kolb — Experiential Learning Theory (ELT)

Concrete experience → reflective observation → abstract conceptualization → active experimentation. Reflection requirements and portfolio evidence ground in Kolb’s cycle.

Coursera 2026 Job Report

Industry job-readiness signals and skills demand from the global learning platform’s 2026 report. Anchors the lifelong-learning & non-credit pillar (200K+ learners on Illinois Tech’s Coursera platform).

Laanan’s Transfer Student Capital

Transfers arrive with accumulated knowledge that predicts persistence and self-efficacy when explicitly surfaced. Core anchor for the Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway.

Schlossberg’s Transition Theory

Situation, Self, Support, Strategies (the 4 S’s). Structures Transfer Cohort coaching, peer community, and transition planning.

Transfer Receptive Culture

Reframes transfer success as an institutional responsibility, not a student responsibility. Drives the Transfer Cohort policy stance.

Elevate Career Readiness Workshop Series

13 workshops · 1.5 hours each · framework-aligned · employment-focused · Fall 2026 & Spring 2027 · detailed resource guide prepared by Dr. Freddrick Logan, PhD · Career Services & Experiential Learning · May 2026.

13
Workshops
1.5h
Each Session (90 min)
8
Frameworks Anchored per Workshop
NACE · P21 · WEF 2030 · AAC&U HIPs · O*NET · CASEL · DOE ESF · Kolb ELT
Fall ’26
Launch Term

Executive Summary

This catalog provides 13 proposed Elevate Career Readiness Workshops designed to ensure Illinois Tech students graduate with the skills, strategies, and confidence to secure employment. Each workshop is 1.5 hours and grounded in all eight foundational career-readiness frameworks — NACE, P21, WEF 2030, AAC&U HIPs, O*NET, CASEL, DOE ESF, and Kolb ELT — with the touched dimensions listed in each workshop card below. Every workshop is directly mapped to Elevate curriculum requirements and the badge ecosystem.

The workshops are informed by curricula from CareerFoundry’s Job Preparation Course (15,500+ career changers), a decade of experiential-learning design, and current labor-market data. They build progressively from foundational planning through advanced negotiation and can be delivered individually or as a complete series. Each entry includes: justification with data, detailed topics, session format, Elevate alignment, badge mapping, and a concrete student takeaway.

Student staff with expertise in financial literacy, AI, and assessments may co-facilitate select workshops, extending delivery capacity without additional cost.

Workshop 1 — Planning Your Job Search

From First Steps to First Offer

Duration1.5 Hours (90 Minutes)
NACE CompetencyCareer & Self-Development
WEF 2030 SkillsActive Learning, Self-Management
P21 4Cs+Critical Thinking, Communication
AAC&U HIPsFirst-Year Experiences (planning anchor); Capstones (multi-year alignment)
O*NET Work ActivitiesUpdating & Using Relevant Knowledge; Working with Computers
CASELSelf-Management, Responsible Decision-Making
DOE ESFWorkplace Skills, Applied Knowledge
Kolb ELTAbstract Conceptualization → Active Experimentation
Elevate BadgesCareer Explorer, Industry Navigator, Job Search Strategist

Why this workshop? Students who begin with a structured plan secure employment 40% faster. Most lack a systematic approach.

What’s covered:

  • Self-assessment: strengths, interests, target roles
  • Building a 30/60/90-day action plan with milestones
  • Tracking systems: spreadsheets, Handshake, CRM tools
  • Identifying target companies by major / domain
  • Job search channels: boards, networking, company sites, recruiters
  • Time management: balancing search with coursework
  • AI-powered job search tools and alerts

Session format (90 min): 20 min instruction · 40 min plan building · 20 min peer review · 10 min wrap-up.

Elevate curriculum alignment: Supports Year 4 requirement of 8 applications / month. Provides organizational infrastructure for that target.

Student outcome: Personalized 90-day action plan, populated tracking spreadsheet, list of 20+ target employers.

Workshop 2 — Converting Projects into Case Studies (CAR Method)

Turn Your Coursework into Career Currency

Duration1.5 Hours (90 Minutes)
NACE CompetencyCommunication, Critical Thinking
WEF 2030 SkillsAnalytical Thinking, Creative Thinking
P21 4Cs+Critical Thinking, Communication
AAC&U HIPsePortfolios; Writing-Intensive Courses
O*NET Work ActivitiesCommunicating with Supervisors / Peers; Documenting / Recording Information
CASELSelf-Awareness (reflection on impact)
DOE ESFApplied Knowledge, Workplace Skills
Kolb ELTReflective Observation → Abstract Conceptualization
Elevate BadgesResume Excellence, Professional Communications L2, Case Study Builder

Why this workshop? Employers report candidates struggle to articulate impact. The CAR (Challenge, Action, Result) method is the most transferable interview and resume skill.

What’s covered:

  • Understanding the CAR framework
  • Identifying which projects and experiences to highlight
  • Quantifying results with metrics
  • Writing CAR statements for resumes, cover letters, portfolios
  • Adapting CAR stories for behavioral interviews (STAR overlap)
  • Live conversion of 2–3 personal projects
  • Peer feedback and refinement

Session format (90 min): 15 min framework · 10 min examples · 40 min writing · 20 min peer critique · 5 min summary.

Elevate curriculum alignment: Enhances Year 1 Resume Development and Year 2 optimization; strengthens Year 3 / 4 interview prep.

Student outcome: 2–3 polished CAR case studies ready for resume, portfolio, and interviews.

Why this workshop? 78% of hiring managers say portfolios influence decisions. Across engineering, computing, and design, employers expect work samples.

What’s covered:

  • Why portfolios matter by industry and major
  • Platforms: GitHub, Behance, WordPress, Canva, Notion, LinkedIn
  • Structure: hero, about, projects, resume, contact
  • Visual storytelling for non-technical audiences
  • Content selection and project descriptions using CAR
  • Live setup of portfolio framework and first project
  • Mobile optimization and accessibility

Session format (90 min): 15 min platforms · 10 min structure · 45 min building · 15 min gallery walk · 5 min next steps.

Elevate curriculum alignment: Supports Year 2 tangible-output requirement; maps to the optional portfolio-creation activity.

Student outcome: Live portfolio framework on chosen platform with at least one completed project.

Workshop 4 — Applying for Jobs with AI

Work Smarter — Using AI Ethically

Duration1.5 Hours (90 Minutes)
NACE CompetencyTechnology, Career & Self-Development
WEF 2030 SkillsAI and Big Data, Technology Literacy
P21 4Cs+Critical Thinking, Information / Media / Technology Literacy
AAC&U HIPsCommon Intellectual Experiences (AI literacy as cross-disciplinary)
O*NET Work ActivitiesWorking with Computers; Analyzing Data / Information; Interacting with Computers
CASELResponsible Decision-Making (ethical AI use), Self-Awareness
DOE ESFApplied Knowledge, Workplace Skills
Kolb ELTActive Experimentation (hands-on tool use)

Why this workshop? AI has changed hiring for both sides. Students who use AI ethically gain advantage; those who misuse it get flagged by ATS.

What’s covered:

  • How employers use AI: ATS, video interviews, assessments
  • Ethical AI use: where the line is
  • Resume tailoring with AI: keyword matching
  • Cover letter drafting: starting points, not final products
  • Company research with AI
  • Interview prep with AI: practice questions and feedback
  • Automated job-search tools
  • What AI platforms actually score you on
  • Common pitfalls: over-reliance, generic outputs, detection

Session format (90 min): 20 min overview · 15 min demos · 35 min exercises · 15 min ethics discussion · 5 min wrap-up.

Elevate curriculum alignment: Supports Year 4 job-application requirements; connects to AWS Academy and AI literacy.

Student outcome: AI-tailored resume for one target job, vetted tool list, clear ethical guidelines.

Why this workshop? 85% of jobs are filled through networking, yet most students have never conducted a single informational interview.

What’s covered:

  • What informational interviews are and why they work
  • Identifying the right people: alumni, professionals, hiring managers
  • Writing outreach that gets responses
  • Questions that build genuine relationships
  • Conducting the conversation: structure, timing, follow-up
  • The 48-hour follow-up rule
  • Converting interviews into referrals
  • Role-play exercise: live informational interview

Session format (90 min): 15 min framework · 10 min outreach writing · 10 min questions · 30 min role-play · 15 min debrief · 10 min wrap-up.

Elevate curriculum alignment: Supports Year 1 mentorship and Year 2 networking; feeds Year 3 / 4 job-search pipeline.

Student outcome: 3 drafted outreach messages, 5 target professionals, question bank ready.

Delivery Model & Fall 2026 Launch Plan

Workshops are branded as Elevate Career Readiness Workshops, open to all students with Elevate cohort priority. Co-facilitation with Career Services coaches (Emily, Nick, Jennifer) and the new coach hire (replacing Bernie) is in scope. Student staff with specialized expertise in financial literacy, AI, and assessments may co-facilitate select workshops — extending delivery capacity without additional cost.

Initial 5-workshop ship plan for Fall 2026 launch:

  • Workshop 3 — Portfolio Development (with Emily · tangible-output focus)
  • Workshop 10 — Remote / Hybrid Work Readiness
  • Workshop 13 — Career Pivoting & Transferable Skills (with Emily)
  • Workshop 9 — Personal Branding for the AI Era
  • Workshop 8 — Salary Negotiation + Offer Evaluation (combined track aligned with CPT)

Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway

A one-year onboarding model that prepares transfer students for the standard Elevate cohorts · Final Draft v1.1 (April 22, 2026) · Summer 2026 build-out for Fall 2026 launch.

You’re Not Behind. You’re Ready.

A dedicated Transfer Cohort — not a parallel track and not a lower bar.

Every transfer student enters for one academic year, completes the nine base activities, and is placed into the standard Y1, Y2, Y3, or Y4 cohort that matches their remaining time to graduation. National research: transfers lose ~50% of earned credits during institutional handoff; only 16% of community-college entrants complete a bachelor’s within six years (9–13% for Black, Hispanic, low-income, and older students). The Elevate guarantee is the same for every student.

Summer 2026 Build-Out · Fall 2026 Launch

The Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway will be built out and finalized over Summer 2026 for the Fall 2026 launch. The summer work includes: finalizing the 9-activity Campus Groups form library (Transfer-specific or Y1-with-Transfer-tag), assigning a Transfer Elevate Success Coach (existing coach or new hire from the 2026–27 cohort manager recruitment), publishing the Activity 3 prior-learning auto-credit policy, adding a Transfer tab to the Elevate Student Activity Tracker alongside Y1 and Y2, aligning the admitted-student flyer campaign with Cynthia, and confirming the pilot enrollment ramp (50–100 transfers = 25–50% of the ~200 incoming per cycle).

9
Base Activities
1 yr

4

Completion Goal

Executive Summary

Transfer students arrive at Illinois Tech with prior college experience, work history, and life context that four-year starters do not have — and often with less time to navigate a brand-new career-readiness system. National research shows transfers lose, on average, nearly half of their earned credits during institutional handoff and are significantly less likely to complete a bachelor’s degree when that happens [Monaghan & Attewell, 2015; Strada, 2023]. Only 16% of community-college entrants transfer and complete a bachelor’s within six years, dropping to 9–13% for Black, Hispanic, low-income, and older students [Velasco, Fink & Jenkins, 2024]. Elevate’s guarantee is the same for every student: complete the requirements and receive an employment offer within six months of graduation, or access tuition-free graduate coursework.

This report proposes a dedicated Transfer Cohort that runs for one year as a structured bridge, then promotes each student into the standard Y1, Y2, Y3, or Y4 cohort that fits their academic standing. The nine bridge-year activities are mapped to the frameworks that already underpin Elevate — NACE Career Readiness Competencies, P21, the WEF Future of Jobs, CASEL SEL, and AAC&U High-Impact Practices — and are additionally grounded in three transfer-specific frameworks: Laanan’s Transfer Student Capital, Schlossberg’s Transition Theory, and the Transfer Receptive Culture framework.

Why a Transfer Cohort?

  • Transfers enroll mid-program, miss Pre-College onboarding, and cannot complete a full Y1 or Y2 calendar in the semesters they have left. Ishitani’s longitudinal work documents a measurable “transfer shock” in GPA and engagement during the first post-transfer term [Ishitani, 2008].
  • Top feeders — City Colleges of Chicago, College of DuPage, Moraine Valley, and Triton — send students with coursework and work experience that deserves credit, not duplication. The CCRC/Aspen Transfer Playbook names tailored transfer advising and prior-learning credit review as essential practices that distinguish high-transfer-success institutions [Wyner et al., 2016; Velasco et al., 2024].
  • A competency-recognition gap exists. NACE’s 2024 Student Survey and Job Outlook 2025 document a persistent disconnect between how students and employers perceive student proficiency — particularly in Career & Self-Development and Leadership [NACE, 2024]. Transfers are disproportionately affected because their prior work and coursework are invisible without deliberate translation.
  • Internal Elevate tracker data shows engagement drops sharply after T&C signing; transfers without a structured on-ramp fall into that gap faster than four-year starters. Transfer students have repeatedly expressed interest in Elevate but been told the initiative was not yet set up for them.
  • The flyer promise to admitted transfers — “You’re Not Behind. You’re Ready.” — needs a curriculum behind it. This report is that curriculum.

Philosophy & Structure

The Transfer Cohort is a one-year bridge — not a parallel track and not a lower bar. Every transfer student enters for one academic year from their start term, regardless of class standing, completes the nine base activities, and is placed into the standard Y1, Y2, Y3, or Y4 cohort that matches their remaining time to graduation.

Three design principles:

  1. Meet transfers where they are [Ishitani, 2008; Schlossberg, 1981].
  2. Credit what they have already done [Monaghan & Attewell, 2015; Laanan et al., 2010].
  3. Never let a transfer reach graduation without a clear path to the employment guarantee [NACE; Kuh, 2008; Jain et al., 2020].

Schlossberg’s moving-in / moving-through / moving-out phases map directly onto the bridge year: orientation for moving-in (Activities 1–2), coach check-ins and peer community for moving-through (Activities 3–8), and Activity 9 transition planning for moving-out into the standard cohort.

The Nine Base Activities

Activities 1–3 happen in the first six weeks · Activities 4–8 across the rest of the year · Activity 9 in the final four weeks before cohort placement. These nine activities replace a transfer student’s Y1 or Y2 required list for the bridge year. Every completion is logged via Campus Groups and justified in the Evidence Log — no exceptions.

# Activity What It Builds / Anchor Evidence Accepted
1Transfer Orientation & T&C AgreementProgram commitment; understanding the employment guarantee. NACE Career & Self-Dev; Schlossberg Moving-In.Signed T&C form on Campus Groups.
2Transfer Elevate Success Coach — First Check-InDedicated transfer coach assigned. Schlossberg 4 S’s; CASEL Self-Awareness.Coach Check-In form submission.
3Prior Learning & Experience AuditMaps prior coursework, internships, work history to Elevate. Laanan TSC; credit portability.Coach-approved Prior Experience Worksheet.
4Handshake Profile + Resume ReviewCareer platform presence; resume optimized. NACE Technology & Communication; WEF Tech Literacy.Handshake screenshot + resume review.
5LinkedIn Profile Creation / OptimizationProfessional brand for transfers with work history. Transfer Receptive Culture; Laanan TSC.Profile screenshot or Career Services review.
6Illinois Tech Community ImmersionBelonging and peer network. AAC&U Learning Communities; CASEL Relationship Skills.RSO roster, club reflection, or Campus Groups org.
7Chicago AdventurePlace-based learning and cultural immersion. AAC&U Diversity & Global Learning; Kolb Concrete Experience.Reflection referencing a Chicago experience.
8Elevate Experience (Official Program Event)Exposure to Elevate programming, partners, community. AAC&U HIPs; NACE Professionalism.Event attendance via Elevate Experience form.
9Transfer-to-Cohort Transition MeetingEnd-of-year placement (Y1, Y2, Y3, or Y4). Schlossberg Moving-Out; CASEL Responsible Decision-Making.Coach-signed Transition Placement Form.

Pathway After Year One — Joining the Standard Cohorts

Transfer → Year 1 (Exposure)

For freshman/sophomore standing (grad year 2029/2028). Completes Y1 Exposure activities: resume, mentorship, Chicago Adventure, student org, Elevate Experience, two electives.

Transfer → Year 2 (Engagement)

For junior standing (grad year 2030/2027). LinkedIn optimization, resume review, Elevate Experience, professional development, leadership activity, two electives.

Transfer → Year 3 (Accountability)

For late-stage transfers whose prior experience already satisfies Y1 + Y2. Moves directly into Y3 with 40+ hours of supervised professional experience.

Transfer → Year 4 (Upskilling, Pilot)

For rare one-year transfers (e.g., graduating seniors). Job search strategy, eight applications/month, continued professional development toward the employment guarantee.

Frameworks & Evidence Base

Career-readiness anchors: NACE Career Readiness, P21 4 Cs, WEF Future of Jobs 2025/2030, CASEL SEL.

High-Impact Practices: AAC&U / Kuh — first-year experiences, learning communities, internships, service learning, capstones (4 embedded).

Transfer-specific research: CCRC/Aspen Transfer Playbook (Wyner et al., 2016); Monaghan & Attewell (2015); Ishitani (2008); Velasco/Fink/Jenkins (2024); Strada Transfer Mobility Report (2023).

Transfer-specific frameworks (new): Laanan’s Transfer Student Capital (Laanan, Starobin & Eggleston, 2010; Lukszo & Hayes, 2020); Schlossberg’s Transition Theory (Schlossberg, 1981); Transfer Receptive Culture (Jain, Bernal Melendez & Herrera, 2020). Pre/post administration of the L-TSQ (Moser, 2012) for validated impact measurement.

Success Metrics & Open Questions

Success Metrics

  • 100% of incoming transfers assigned a Transfer Elevate Success Coach within 2 weeks of enrollment.
  • 85%+ of Transfer Cohort students complete the 9 base activities within their bridge year.
  • Signed Transition Placement Form on file for every transfer before their second academic year.
  • Transfer graduation-to-employment rate meets or exceeds the broader Elevate benchmark and the national community-college-to-bachelor’s rate.
  • Pre/post L-TSQ to measure change in transfer student capital across the bridge year.

Open Questions (Resolved Summer 2026)

  • Transfer-specific Campus Groups forms, or reuse Y1 forms with a transfer tag?
  • Should Activity 3 auto-credit standard Elevate activities, or route every decision through the coach?
  • Who staffs the Transfer Elevate Success Coach role — existing coaches, or a new hire from the 2026–27 cohort manager recruitment?
  • Pilot with Fall 2026 transfers, or summer pilot with returning transfers to pressure-test the 9-activity list first?

Electives, Evidence, and Flexibility

Transfer Cohort students may bank elective credits during the bridge year from the 70+ optional experiences cataloged on elevate.iit.edu — Hawks in Action athletics, Career Fair Prep Squad, Student Organization Leadership, VMock AI Resume Review, AWS Cloud Skills Badges, Community Impact (Bronzeville Service), Wild Card / Elevator Pitch, and more.

These electives are explicitly mapped to NACE and WEF skill demands so students can articulate them in interviews. Electives earned during the bridge year carry forward into the standard cohort the student enters.

Evidence rules follow the existing Elevate Tracker standard: every completion is logged via Campus Groups and justified in the Evidence Log — no exceptions.

Summer 2026 Build-Out Plan · Fall 2026 Launch Schedule

A week-by-week buildout from May 2026 acceptance through the Fall 2026 launch window. The plan is sequenced so the 9-activity form library, the Transfer Elevate Success Coach assignment, and the admitted-student flyer campaign all land before the first incoming transfer arrives in August.

WindowBuild-Out WorkstreamOwner
May 2026Team review & sign-off on Final Draft v1.1; align with Cynthia on admitted-transfer flyer campaign messaging.Freddrick + Cynthia
June 2026Build the 9-activity Campus Groups form library; decide Transfer-specific forms vs. Y1-with-Transfer-tag; add Transfer tab to Elevate Student Activity Tracker.Career Services + 312 / Ready Education
June 2026Confirm Transfer Elevate Success Coach staffing — existing-coach reassignment or new 2026–27 cohort manager hire (Danish Nadar / Rabiraj Kannan in pipeline).Freddrick + Yogesh handoff
June 2026Publish the 9-activity sequence on the Elevate source-of-truth dashboard (this page).Dashboard team
July 2026Activity 3 Prior Learning & Experience Audit policy finalized: auto-credit rules vs. coach-routed decisions; published worksheet template.Freddrick + Coach team
July 2026Optional summer pressure-test with returning transfers (5–10 students) to validate the 9-activity list before scaled launch.Freddrick
July–August 2026Admitted-transfer flyer campaign distribution; L-TSQ pre-administration tooling stood up.Cynthia + Admissions
August 2026Transfer Elevate Success Coach onboarding; coach trained on 9-activity sequence and the Schlossberg / Laanan TSC / Transfer Receptive Culture frameworks.Coach team
Fall 2026 (Aug–Dec)LAUNCH. First Transfer Cohort enrolls; Activities 1–3 complete in first six weeks; pilot target 50–100 transfers.Full Career Services team
Spring 2027Activities 4–8 across the semester; mid-year cohort engagement check.Coach team
April–May 2027Activity 9 transition meetings; L-TSQ post-administration; first cohort placed into Y1 / Y2 / Y3 / Y4 standard cohorts.Coach team + Freddrick

Next Steps

Immediate. Team review of this draft · alignment with Cynthia and the admitted-student flyer campaign · publishing the 9-activity sequence on the Elevate source-of-truth dashboard (this page) · adding a Transfer tab to the Elevate Student Activity Tracker alongside the existing Y1 and Y2 staff assignment sheets. Pilot enrollment target: 50–100 transfers (25–50% of the ~200 incoming per cycle).

Frameworks Cited in Building This Bridge Pathway

Every element of the Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway is anchored to externally validated frameworks so the model is defensible to academic partners, accreditors, and employers. The 9-activity sequence, the four-track placement model, the Activity 3 prior-learning audit, and the L-TSQ pre/post measurement protocol all derive from the sources below.

FrameworkCitationHow It Shapes the Bridge Pathway
NACE Career Readiness CompetenciesNACE, 2024 [1]The 8 competencies (Communication, Critical Thinking, Teamwork, Professionalism, Technology, Leadership, Career & Self-Development, Equity & Inclusion) anchor Activities 1, 4, 7, 8.
P21 Framework for 21st Century LearningBattelle for Kids, 2019 [2]The 4 Cs (Critical Thinking, Communication, Collaboration, Creativity) underpin Activity 6 Community Immersion and Activity 8 Elevate Experience.
WEF Future of Jobs 2025World Economic Forum, 2025 [3]Top 15 skills for 2025–2030 inform Activities 4 (Handshake / resume optimization for emerging-skill alignment) and 5 (LinkedIn narrative).
CASEL Social-Emotional LearningCASEL, 2023 [8]5 SEL competencies (Self-Awareness, Self-Management, Social Awareness, Relationship Skills, Responsible Decision-Making) guide Activity 2 coach check-ins and Activity 9 transition meetings.
AAC&U High-Impact PracticesKuh, 2008 [7]First-year experiences, learning communities, internships, service learning, capstones — the bridge year embeds 4 HIPs in a single cohort.
Kolb’s Experiential Learning CycleKolb (referenced via [7])Concrete Experience → Reflective Observation → Abstract Conceptualization → Active Experimentation. Activity 7 Chicago Adventure reflection anchors to this cycle.
CCRC / Aspen Transfer PlaybookWyner et al., 2016 [4]Names tailored transfer advising, prior-learning review, and program-of-study alignment as essential practices — the operational scaffold for Activities 1–3.
Tracking Transfer (CCRC / Aspen / NSC)Velasco, Fink & Jenkins, 2024 [9]National benchmarks for community-college-to-bachelor’s completion rates — sets the 85%+ Transfer Cohort completion goal against an external standard.
Credit Loss & Bachelor’s CompletionMonaghan & Attewell, 2015 [5]Documents ~50% transfer credit loss during institutional handoff — the empirical case for Activity 3 Prior Learning & Experience Audit.
Transfer MobilityStrada Education Foundation, 2023 [10]Credit-portability data informing the Prior Learning & Experience Audit worksheet.
Transfer Shock (Ishitani)Ishitani, 2008 [6]Documents the measurable GPA + engagement drop in the first post-transfer term — justifies the front-loaded Activities 1–3 in the first six weeks.
Laanan’s Transfer Student Capital (TSC)Laanan, Starobin & Eggleston, 2010 [12]; Lukszo & Hayes, 2020 [15]Core anchor for the asset-based framing of transfers; informs Activities 3 and 5 (LinkedIn narrative surfaces accumulated work history).
Schlossberg’s Transition TheorySchlossberg, 1981 [13]Moving-In / Moving-Through / Moving-Out and the 4 S’s (Situation, Self, Support, Strategies) structure the bridge-year arc: Activities 1–2 (Moving-In), 3–8 (Moving-Through), 9 (Moving-Out).
Transfer Receptive CultureJain, Bernal Melendez & Herrera, 2020 [14]Reframes transfer success as an institutional responsibility, not a student responsibility — the cultural ethos behind “You’re Not Behind. You’re Ready.”
L-TSQ Validated InstrumentMoser, 2012 [16]The Laanan-Transfer Students’ Questionnaire is the validated pre/post measure of change in transfer student capital across the bridge year — the program-evaluation backbone.

Cross-Functional Partners — Who We Need to Work Closely With

The Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway cannot launch successfully from Career Services alone. The Summer 2026 build-out window depends on close, named collaboration with the Transfer Office, Admissions, and Markie Lin Rhodes (Director, Undergraduate Admission). Each owns a distinct piece of the transfer-student journey upstream of Elevate, and each is essential to a complete, branded, on-time Fall 2026 launch.

Transfer Office

  • Transfer student roster and matriculation timeline (acceptance → deposit → orientation → first class).
  • Existing prior-learning credit-evaluation workflow — the Activity 3 Prior Learning & Experience Audit must complement, not duplicate, the Transfer Office’s credit-articulation process.
  • Top-feeder relationships (City Colleges of Chicago, College of DuPage, Moraine Valley, Triton) for upstream awareness building.
  • Transfer-specific orientation week programming — Activity 1 (Orientation & T&C Agreement) embeds into the existing Transfer Orientation rather than adding a competing event.

Admissions

  • Admitted-transfer contact list and opt-in pipeline for pre-matriculation outreach (May–August 2026 window).
  • Yield-protection messaging — the “You’re Not Behind. You’re Ready.” flyer needs to land in the admit packet, not after deposit.
  • Counselor talking points so Admissions counselors can describe Elevate + the Transfer Cohort in recruitment conversations and at feeder-school visits.
  • Joint reporting on transfer yield, deposit conversion, and first-term retention so the Transfer Cohort can be evaluated against Admissions’ own KPIs.

Markie Lin Rhodes — Director, Undergraduate Admission

mrhodes1@illinoistech.edu · iit.edu/directory/people/markie-rhodes

  • Brand-consistent design and approval of the Transfer Cohort flyer, web hub (elevate.iit.edu/transfer), and Optional Experiences community-filter pill.
  • Photography and testimonial collection from current transfer students for an authentic asset library.
  • Social and email campaign for the summer admit window, plus a launch announcement timed to Fall 2026.
  • Coordination on the cross-feeder digital campaign (City Colleges of Chicago, College of DuPage, Moraine Valley, Triton) — ideally paid + earned + community placements running through Summer 2026.
  • Alignment with Admissions counselor-facing materials so the “You’re Not Behind. You’re Ready.” promise lives inside the admit packet, not after deposit.

Working-Group Cadence — Summer 2026

A standing weekly cross-functional working group is proposed for June through August 2026, chaired by Dr. Freddrick Logan with named representatives from the Transfer Office, Admissions, and Markie Lin Rhodes (Director, Undergraduate Admission). Meeting deliverables: confirmed admit-list pipeline (Admissions), finalized prior-learning workflow integration (Transfer Office), branded flyer + web hub + social assets (Markie Rhodes), and a green-lit Fall 2026 launch checklist signed off by all four partners before the August matriculation window opens.

References (APA 7)

[1] National Association of Colleges and Employers. (2024). Career readiness competencies for college graduates. NACE.

[2] Partnership for 21st Century Learning / Battelle for Kids. (2019). Framework for 21st century learning definitions.

[3] World Economic Forum. (2025). The future of jobs report 2025. Geneva, Switzerland.

[4] Wyner, J., Deane, K. C., Jenkins, D., & Fink, J. (2016). The transfer playbook: Essential practices for two- and four-year colleges. The Aspen Institute College Excellence Program & Community College Research Center.

[5] Monaghan, D. B., & Attewell, P. (2015). The community college route to the bachelor’s degree. Educational Evaluation and Policy Analysis, 37(1), 70–91.

[6] Ishitani, T. T. (2008). How do transfers survive after “transfer shock”? A longitudinal study of transfer student departure at a four-year institution. Research in Higher Education, 49(5), 403–419.

[7] Kuh, G. D. (2008). High-impact educational practices: What they are, who has access to them, and why they matter. AAC&U.

[8] Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning. (2023). CASEL’s SEL framework.

[9] Velasco, T., Fink, J., & Jenkins, D. (2024). Tracking transfer: Community college and four-year institutional effectiveness in broadening bachelor’s degree attainment. CCRC, Aspen Institute, NSC.

[10] Strada Education Foundation. (2023). Transfer mobility report: How well do college credits transfer across U.S. institutions?

[11] Illinois Tech Elevate Initiative. (2026). Elevate Student Activity Tracker — internal Campus Groups evidence data.

[12] Laanan, F. S., Starobin, S. S., & Eggleston, L. E. (2010). Adjustment of community college students at a four-year university: Role and relevance of transfer student capital for student retention. Journal of College Student Retention, 12(2), 175–209.

[13] Schlossberg, N. K. (1981). A model for analyzing human adaptation to transition. The Counseling Psychologist, 9(2), 2–18.

[14] Jain, D., Bernal Melendez, S. N., & Herrera, A. R. (2020). Power to the transfer: Critical race theory and a transfer receptive culture. Michigan State University Press.

[15] Lukszo, C. M., & Hayes, S. (2020). Facilitating transfer student success: Exploring sources of transfer student capital. Community College Review, 48(1), 31–54.

[16] Moser, K. M. (2012). Redefining transfer student success: Transfer capital and the Laanan-Transfer Students’ Questionnaire (L-TSQ) revisited [Doctoral dissertation, Iowa State University].

Center for Learning Innovation (CLI)

The natural badge-system partner for Elevate’s university-wide expansion · Joint roadmap with CLI Managing Director Lauren Woods

Post-meeting follow-up · May 2026
“

Five concrete collaboration threads surfaced at the Lauren Woods sync — sequenced for summer execution.

Post-meeting note sent to Lauren (May 2026) lays out the prioritization: (1) Ed2Go × Elevate integration moving over the summer, (2) 312 as partial alternative to Credly for the internal audience + retained Credly for external + an MIT Digital Credentials Consortium future-state conversation with Norma, (3) faculty / staff badging through 312, (4) Faculty Summer Camp + Learning Innovation Symposium participation, and (5) Talon platform / Kaplan loop-in for credentialing-standards alignment. Suggested next step: a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks, ideally with Norma, to prioritize sequencing and lock the badging framework before fall.

Why CLI? Elevate’s digital badge system was developed in Career Services and is ready to scale beyond it. CLI — which recently absorbed the Office of Professional and Continuing Education (OPCE) — is the institutional home for transcript integration, AI-powered badge recommendations, alumni badge networks, and the Open Badges 3.0 / Verifiable Credentials roadmap. CLI currently uses Credly for badging (over their seat limits and expensive) and just launched Ed2Go for non-credit professional courses with revenue-share — both create immediate Elevate integration opportunities.

Five Collaboration Threads — Post-Meeting Roadmap (May 2026)

Distilled from the May 2026 post-meeting follow-up letter to Lauren Woods. Each thread carries an owner, a status, an immediate ask, and a sequencing target so the joint roadmap is decision-ready before the summer execution window opens.

PRIORITY 1 · IMMEDIATE

1. Ed2Go × Elevate integration

Integrate Ed2Go courses into Elevate’s optional / elective tracks so students earn an Ed2Go completion and a stackable Elevate career-readiness badge. Gives Ed2Go a built-in promotional channel through Elevate’s 398 enrolled students (with Junior cohort + Transfer pilot launching this fall), while reinforcing that career readiness is grounded in evidence-based skill development.

Owner. Fred Logan (Career Services / Elevate)
Ask. Lauren’s green light to draft a short integration plan.
Target. Summer 2026 execution.

STRATEGIC · HYBRID PROPOSED

2. The Credly question — 312 as a partial alternative

312 (CampusGroups) is part of the institutional subscription, fully automated, supports stackable progressions plus LinkedIn sharing — strong fit for internal Illinois Tech audiences (students, faculty, staff) and would meaningfully reduce CLI’s Credly overage. Honest constraint: 312 doesn’t yet have Credly’s public sharing surface (embeddable links, public verification URLs, downloadable certificates). For the Coursera / India partner work and external learners, 312 alone may not be the right vehicle.

Recommendation: Hybrid — 312 internal, Credly retained for external partners. Future-state conversation: MIT Digital Credentials Consortium for the maturing credentialing strategy.

Owner. Lauren Woods + Norma (decision-makers)
Ask. Sequence the hybrid; loop in Norma on MIT DCC future-state.
Target. Decision before fall.

SUPPORTED · READY TO SCOPE

3. Faculty / staff badging development

Fully supported on 312. New Faculty Orientation, AI for Faculty Workshops, Summer Camp, and the Learning Innovation Symposium can all carry CLI-issued badges with the same automation Elevate uses today. Reuses the verified badge architecture without a new tool stack.

Owner. Fred Logan (framework scoping)
Ask. Lauren’s sign-off to begin scoping.
Target. Fall 2026 launch with New Faculty Orientation.

INVITATION ACCEPTED

4. Faculty Summer Camp + Learning Innovation Symposium

Three contribution modes on the table: session presenter on Elevate’s badging framework, member of the steering committee with Lauren and Mohammed, or behind-the-scenes coordination. The “designing for the future” theme aligns directly with the AI-era career readiness work Elevate is doing.

Owner. Fred Logan + Mohammed (programming)
Ask. Pick contribution mode; align session concepts.
Target. Confirm role before camp planning closes.

COORDINATE EARLY

5. Talon platform / Kaplan loop-in

Early coordination so badge metadata, skills attribution, and credentialing standards align across Elevate, CLI, and the Talon platform from the start — not bolted on after launch.

Owner. Fred Logan (first step: connect with Kaplan)
Ask. Lauren + Norma looped in once something concrete is on the table.
Target. Initial Kaplan touchpoint before the 30-min Norma follow-up.

NEXT STEP

30-min follow-up with Lauren + Norma

In two weeks (ideally with Norma if her schedule allows) to prioritize which threads to move first. Two specific outcomes Fred has named:

  1. Get Ed2Go × Elevate moving over the summer.
  2. Lock the badging framework before fall.

Conversation also opens prep work for Summer Camp, Symposium, and other CLI summer events.

Joint Roadmap — Sequenced Across Summer 2026

WindowActionOwnerOutcome

Late May / June 2026Draft Ed2Go × Elevate integration plan; first Kaplan / Talon touchpoint; initial badging-framework scope for faculty / staffFred LoganDrafts circulated to Lauren + Norma; Kaplan response received
