Decision-Ready Asks — What Lauren and Norma Are Being Asked To Decide

  1. Approve Ed2Go × Elevate draft integration plan. Outcome: stackable badge + Ed2Go completion paths live by end of summer.
  2. Confirm the hybrid 312 / Credly model. Outcome: internal CLI badging migrates to 312, freeing Credly seats for external partner programs.
  3. Open the MIT Digital Credentials Consortium future-state conversation with Norma. Outcome: clarity on whether DCC anchors the next-phase credentialing strategy.
  4. Designate Fred’s Summer Camp + Symposium contribution mode. Outcome: session, steering, or coordination slot confirmed.
  5. Authorize the first Kaplan / Talon touchpoint so badge metadata standards align across Elevate, CLI, and the Talent platform from launch.

Key CLI Contacts

NameRole
Lauren WoodsManaging Director, CLI — primary collaborator on Elevate badge expansion
NormaCLI decision-maker; in conversation with Kaplan on Talent platform integration
MohammedFaculty Summer Camp co-organizer; Learning Innovation Symposium lead

Joint Initiative Roadmap with CLI

University-Wide Badge Ecosystem

Expand the badge framework beyond Career Services to encompass academic departments, student affairs, and co-curricular programs. CLI is the natural orchestrator.

Transcript Integration

Working with the Registrar to add badge achievements to the official academic transcript or a co-curricular transcript. CLI co-leads the policy + technical pathway.

AI-Powered Badge Recommendations

Machine-learned, personalized badge pathways based on student major, career goals, and current progress. Connects to the Chiron AI avatar work CLI has been piloting.

Open Badges 3.0 / Verifiable Credentials

Implement the Open Badges 3.0 standard so badges are machine-readable and employer-verifiable. Aligns with MIT Digital Credentials Consortium research.

Employer Badge Dashboard

Recruiter-facing portal where employers can filter candidates by badges earned — transparent verified competencies for hiring partners.

Student-Facing Badge Portfolio

Shareable URL portfolio so students can show progress, see what’s remaining, and present competencies to employers and graduate programs.

Alumni Badge Network

Allow alumni to continue earning badges post-graduation through professional development and mentorship engagement. Continuous lifelong-learning loop.

Stacked Badge Automation

Automated triggers for stacked badges (Future CEO, Future CMO, etc.) when students complete the curated activity sequence — eliminating manual review.

Simplicity Platform Migration

Evaluating migration from Campus Groups (312) to Simplicity for badge management, automated issuance triggers, and improved analytics.

CLI is Building Their Own Badge Library Around CLI Programming

Per the May 2026 sync with Lauren Woods, CLI is not adopting the Elevate badge catalog verbatim. Instead, CLI will develop its own badge library centered on CLI’s programming portfolio — New Faculty Orientation, AI for Faculty Workshops, the Learning Innovation Symposium, the Faculty Summer Camp, Ed2Go non-credit pathways, and the future Coursera / India partner work. Career Services will share the Elevate framework, verification standards, and 312 automation patterns so CLI can stand up its own catalog quickly without re-inventing the architecture.

What Career Services contributes: framework grounding (8 nationally-recognized frameworks), badge metadata standards, the 312 automation playbook, naming conventions, color-category guidance, and verification-method patterns (automatic / portfolio / external / peer). What CLI defines: badge titles, learning outcomes, hour thresholds, and the program-specific competencies that reflect CLI’s mission — faculty development, continuing education, AI literacy across the institution, and non-credit professional pathways.

Introducing the MIT Digital Credentials Consortium (DCC) to Illinois Tech

Future-state credentialing · strategic introduction

A research-grade, vendor-neutral path beyond Credly — cofounded by MIT, Berkeley, Harvard, and a global university consortium.

As CLI scales its credentialing footprint and Career Services builds out the Elevate badge library, the long-horizon question is: which credentialing infrastructure will higher education converge on? The MIT Digital Credentials Consortium (DCC) is the most credible answer being developed today — a non-profit, research-grade consortium building the open-source infrastructure for verifiable, portable, learner-owned digital credentials at scale.

digitalcredentials.mit.edu →

What the Digital Credentials Consortium is

The DCC is a global consortium of leading universities and research institutions building the technical and governance infrastructure for verifiable digital credentials — credentials a learner can hold, share, and verify without depending on a single vendor or platform. Founded in 2018, hosted at MIT Open Learning, with founding partners including UC Berkeley, Harvard University, Georgia Tech, Cornell, the University of California system, TU Munich, Delft, the Hasso Plattner Institute, and McMaster University, among others.

DCC is not a credentialing platform Illinois Tech would license. It is the open-source infrastructure layer — specifications, reference implementations, governance — that platforms like Credly, 312, and Talon could adopt to interoperate with each other and with employer / graduate-school verification systems.

Why DCC matters for Illinois Tech — six structural advantages

Learner-owned, portable

A student carries their credentials in their own wallet, not inside a vendor’s database. If Credly, 312, or any platform sunsets a program, the credential persists.

Vendor-neutral interoperability

A DCC-aligned credential issued through 312 verifies the same way as one from Credly, MIT, or Berkeley. Solves the proliferating-platform problem Illinois Tech already faces.

W3C Verifiable Credentials standard

Built on the W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model — the global standard now adopted across higher education, government, and employer ecosystems.

Open-source, no vendor lock-in

DCC reference implementations (issuer-coordinator, wallet, verifier) are open-source under MIT / Apache licenses. Illinois Tech retains full control.

Privacy-preserving by design

A learner can prove they hold a credential without revealing additional personal data. Solves FERPA-adjacent concerns when credentials cross institutional boundaries.

Future-proofs the credential strategy

Talon, Credly, 312 are infrastructure choices today. DCC alignment is what keeps tomorrow’s infrastructure choice from invalidating every credential Illinois Tech has issued.

DCC at a glance — key facts and references

TopicDetailReference
HomeHosted at MIT Open Learning; founded 2018digitalcredentials.mit.edu
StandardW3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model + Open Badges 3.0 alignmentw3.org/TR/vc-data-model
Open-source codeReference implementations: issuer-coordinator, learner wallet, verifier-appgithub.com/digitalcredentials
Learner WalletFree, learner-controlled credential wallet (iOS / Android / Web)lcw.app
Verifier appPublic-facing verifier for employers / graduate schools to validate any DCC-issued credentialverifierplus.org
Founding membersMIT · UC Berkeley · Harvard · Georgia Tech · Cornell · UC system · TU Munich · TU Delft · Hasso Plattner Institute · McMaster (representative subset)about page
GovernanceNon-profit, vendor-neutral, member-governed; quarterly all-member meetings; technical working groupsgovernance
Reporting + researchPublished white papers on credential portability, infrastructure design, and equity-by-design credential systemspapers & reports
Membership costTiered annual membership; non-profit research consortium pricing (not a per-credential vendor fee)membership info
ContactDirect contact for membership / partnership inquiriesdigitalcredentials-info@mit.edu

How DCC fits with Illinois Tech’s current credentialing stack

LayerCurrent role at Illinois TechHow DCC fits
312 (CampusGroups)Internal issuer for Elevate badges (institutional subscription, automation, LinkedIn sharing)312 can adopt DCC-aligned issuance so its badges become interoperable with external verifiers without losing internal automation.
CredlyCLI’s external-facing issuer (currently over seat limits / cost)Credly already supports OpenBadges 3.0 and is moving toward Verifiable Credentials. DCC alignment lets a Credly badge verify on any DCC verifier — retains external partner value while reducing lock-in.
TalonLong-range talent / credential platform (Kaplan-adjacent; on the Elevate roadmap)Best implemented DCC-native from launch so Talon credentials can be verified by employers using the same public verifier as MIT, Berkeley, and other DCC institutions.
Ed2GoNon-credit course completion + Elevate badge stack (Summer 2026 integration)Ed2Go completions can be issued as DCC-aligned credentials so non-credit pathways carry the same verifiability weight as for-credit Illinois Tech credentials.
Open Badges 3.0Underlying spec on the Elevate badge architecture roadmapOpen Badges 3.0 is the bridge: it sits on top of W3C Verifiable Credentials — the same standard DCC built on. Adopting OB 3.0 is the first step toward DCC alignment.

Recommended engagement paths — from awareness to membership

STEP 1 · NOW

Briefing for Lauren + Norma

15-min walkthrough of this page + the DCC overview. Captures shared vocabulary so the hybrid 312 / Credly decision and future Talon scoping happen with DCC in mind.

STEP 2 · SUMMER 2026

Senior-leadership briefing

Brief to BJ Engelhardt and Mallik Sundharam on the strategic value of DCC alignment for Illinois Tech’s 73-badge architecture + the Workforce + Academic Hybrid Platform vision.

STEP 3 · FALL 2026

Pilot DCC verifier on one Elevate badge

Pick one high-volume badge (e.g., Initiative Taker, AWS Cloud Foundations). Issue a parallel DCC-verifiable version via the open-source issuer-coordinator. Prove portability with a public verifier link.

STEP 4 · SPRING 2027

Evaluate DCC membership

If the pilot validates the value proposition, evaluate full DCC consortium membership. Positions Illinois Tech among the institutional leaders setting credentialing standards for the next decade.

STEP 5 · AY 2027–2028

Full DCC alignment

312 issues DCC-aligned by default; Credly external badges DCC-verifiable; Talon launches DCC-native; Ed2Go completions issued as Verifiable Credentials. End-state: every Illinois Tech credential is portable, learner-owned, and globally verifiable.

Strategic narrative for the university audience

Illinois Tech’s founding charter — making engineers, architects, and physicists of the children of Chicago meatpackers and machinists — rests on the idea that what a student learns is more important than where they learned it. DCC operationalizes that idea for the credentialing era. A DCC-aligned Illinois Tech credential is portable across employers, graduate schools, and international borders. It is verifiable without permission from a vendor. It is owned by the learner.

For CLI specifically, DCC alignment means non-credit Ed2Go completions, faculty development credentials, and Coursera / India partner work all carry the same verifiability weight as a 4-year degree transcript. For Elevate, DCC alignment means every badge a student earns can travel with them for the rest of their career.

Armour College of Engineering × Elevate

Engineering students represent 62% of Y1 co-curricular plan participants — Armour is Elevate’s largest single discipline footprint and the natural home for the engineering buildout of the platform.

In-flight · May 2026
“

Finalization meeting with Armour at the end of May 2026 — pilot updates landing June 2026.

End-of-May working session with Melanie Jones (Senior Professional Academic Advisor, Armour) and Michael Arnaudo (Armour Academy) closes out the design phase for engineering-specific pilot activities and mentorship-program rollout. June 2026 publishes the finalized pilot scope to the dashboard and to 312 — including IPRO Innovation Day alignment, AWS Academy cohort cadence, faculty-mentored research surfaces in the monthly newsletter, and any IPRO badge auto-trigger changes. Watch this page in early June for the finalized pilot release notes.

Engineering-built workforce technology

Per strategic direction, Elevate is being built out with engineering partners as a Workforce + Academic Hybrid Platform Product. APIs and a Talon-based backend are on the long-term roadmap, with the new India development team scoped to scale beyond the current single-architect build. Armour faculty, IPRO project teams, and student engineers (incl. AWS Academy + Cloud Architect cohorts) are first-class technology contributors.

Engineering-Specific Badge & Activity Pathways

AWS Cloud Skills Bootcamp

Free AWS certifications via AWS Academy. Foundational tracks: Cloud Foundations, AI Foundations, Machine Learning Foundations, Cloud Security Foundations. Advanced: Cloud Architecting, Cloud Operations, Data Engineering, NLP, Security Engineering. Badges: Digital Literacy L3–L4, Cloud Foundations.

IPRO Innovation Day

Required Y2 activity. Cross-disciplinary project teams. Badges: Leadership Development L2–L3, Innovation Pioneer.

Hackathons & Startup Studio

Build-week sprints. Kaplan Institute / Startup Studio integration. Badges: Innovation Pioneer, Teamwork (Leadership L1–L3), Career Critical Thinker.

Engineering Research Opportunities

Faculty-mentored undergraduate research. Surfaced via Armour outreach into the monthly Elevate newsletter, contact persons assigned per project area.

Game Design Foundations

Y2 elective; bridges to Esports & Digital Arts badge track. Badges: Digital Literacy L2, Innovation Pioneer.

Industry Research Deep Dive

Required Y2 activity. Engineering students map industry trends to career pathways. Badges: Career Critical Thinker L2–L3.

AWS Academy Scaling Plan (Summer 2026 onward)

Operating model: Three co-leads (Frederick / Career Services · James / ITM faculty · Jeremy / ITM department) plus rolling cohorts. Summer 2026 runs 4-week cohorts to test the rhythm; fall and spring shift to 8-week cohorts aligned with the academic calendar. Goal: 100 students per cohort, with continuous enrollment across foundation and advanced tracks.

Wednesday office hours at the Smart Lab (Kaplan Institute) provide synchronous troubleshooting. A commitment clause at sign-up sets expectations and protects program reputation with AWS.

Outreach scope: Armour, Stuart School of Business, Computing, Design, Architecture — coordinated through department chairs and the monthly Elevate newsletter.

Stuart School of Business × Elevate

The most comprehensive school-level integration in the Elevate ecosystem · 18 business-focused activities across four engagement tiers

The C-Suite Pathway (Black & Gold series). Stuart students complete a curated business sequence to earn stacked C-suite badges — tied to industry certifications, AMA chapter engagement, and Stuart CMC coaching. Braven sits inside the Elevate catalog as a Year 2 Engagement experience, complementing the Stuart pathway.

Partnership Principles (per Curt A., Oct 2025)

  • CMC is promoted to Stuart undergraduates as a partner to Career Services and Elevate.
  • Stuart is aware of data collected and insights uncovered through student participation.
  • Students receive credit for using CMC services and attending CMC events in addition to Career Services events.
  • Stuart sent its own Elevate enrollment announcement with CMC references, co-signed with BJ and Cynthia.
  • Stuart UGs receive credit for attending Stuart CMC events in addition to Career Services events.
  • Stuart requested access to data collected during Elevate Success Coach meetings with Stuart students (FERPA-compliant aggregate).

C-Suite Pathway — Black & Gold Series

Future CEO

Any Stuart Professional Certificate + Entrepreneurial Venture Development + Leadership L3+.

Future COO

Stuart Certificate + Leadership role in Stuart org + Collaboration L3+. Auto-earn: CAPM Certification (PMI).

Future CFO

Stuart Certificate + Stuart Investments Membership + Financial Literacy. Auto-earn: SIE Exam 70%+.

Future CTO

Stuart Professional Certificate (ITM focus) + Tech+ project + ONE of: AWS Cert, Coding Project, ITM/Computing Org.

Future CMO

AMA Membership + Professional Communications L3+ + Marketing Analytics Project.

PeriodMilestone

Key Selling Points for Stuart

Strategic differentiators that make the Elevate × Stuart partnership uniquely valuable in the Chicago business-school market.

Competitive Differentiation

Stuart becomes the only Chicago business school offering guaranteed professional development with measurable badge outcomes tied to NACE career readiness competencies. No other Chicago business program provides a structured, badge-verified co-curricular framework of this scope.

Faculty Integration

Faculty can embed Elevate activities directly into course requirements — students earn academic credit and digital badges simultaneously. One activity, two credentials. Example: a finance professor makes the Financial Literacy Workshop Series a course deliverable; students earn a grade and an Elevate badge.

Promotes Stuart Certificates

Elevate promotes Stuart’s certificate programs (Finance, Marketing, Foundations of Business, Quantitative Methods, Economics) to all Illinois Tech students — not just Stuart students. This expands awareness and enrollment for Stuart programs across the entire university.

Tech+ Alignment

Stuart’s unique Tech+ degree programs (Business & Engineering, Business & IT) are a natural fit for cross-school interdisciplinary badges with Computing. The Tech+ Integration Specialist activity was designed specifically to serve these students and connects directly to the Future CTO pathway.

What Elevate Gives Stuart Students

Every Stuart undergraduate enrolled in Elevate receives the following benefits at no additional cost.

  • Dedicated Elevate Success Coach. Personalized advising from an Elevate Success Coach who understands Stuart’s programs and career landscape.
  • Co-Curricular Plan. A customized career readiness plan developed in the first coach meeting, identifying goals, target activities, and timelines.
  • Access to 80+ Activities. All core Elevate activities plus 18 Stuart-specific electives, plus partnership activities with AWS Academy, Kaplan Institute, and more.
  • Digital Badge Portfolio. Portable, verifiable digital credentials (Open Badges 3.0 / W3C Verifiable Credentials) mapped to NACE competencies and employer expectations.
  • C-Suite Pathway Access. Stuart students are the only students eligible for the Black & Gold C-Suite badges (Future CEO, COO, CFO, CTO, CMO).
  • Employment Guarantee Eligibility. Students who complete all required activities and badges qualify for the Elevate employment guarantee.
  • Stuart CMC Credit. CMC appointments and Stuart events count toward Elevate completion — students don’t have to choose between CMC and Career Services.

What CMC & Stuart Leadership Gets

  • Student Engagement Data. Aggregate data on Stuart student participation in Elevate activities, badge completion rates, and engagement patterns (FERPA-safe).
  • Elevate Success Coach Insights. Themes and trends from Elevate Success Coach meetings with Stuart students — FERPA-compliant aggregate data, not individual records.
  • Cross-Promotion. Elevate actively promotes Stuart certificate programs, CMC events, and Stuart career fairs to the entire Elevate student population — all 398 enrolled students, not just Stuart enrollees.
  • Badge Framework. Stuart can propose new badges at any time. Per the Career Program Planning Meeting: creating new badges is lightweight — define the competency, define the earning criteria, and we implement. Five or ten new Stuart badges can be live within a week.
  • Stuart Resume Template Integration. VMock feedback is calibrated to Stuart’s specific resume guidelines, ensuring students produce Stuart-compliant resumes before earning the badge.

Talking Points for Stuart Walkthrough

A guide for walking Stuart stakeholders through the partnership during meetings, advisory sessions, or recruitment conversations.

Opening Frame

  • Elevate is not a competing program to CMC — it’s an amplifier. CMC and Career Services are partners in serving Stuart students.
  • Stuart has the deepest school-level integration of any college at Illinois Tech: 18 custom activities, 5 C-Suite badges, full CMC event credit.
  • Everything we’ve built is grounded in nationally recognized frameworks — not opinions. NACE, WEF, P21, CASEL, AAC&U, O*NET, DOE, Kolb.

The “Why Should Stuart Care” Pitch

  • Competitive edge: only Chicago business school with guaranteed, badge-verified professional development.
  • Recruitment tool: prospective students and parents can see exactly what career readiness skills their tuition buys.
  • Employer signal: badges provide a standardized, verifiable way for employers to evaluate Stuart grads beyond GPA and major.
  • Course embedding: faculty deliverable counts as both grade and Elevate badge.
  • Cross-college promotion: Stuart certificates promoted to all 398 enrolled students across every college.

Addressing Common Questions

QuestionResponse
How do Stuart students join?They enroll through the same Elevate portal (elevate.iit.edu). Stuart sent its own enrollment communication co-signed by BJ and Cynthia. Stuart students in Elevate are automatically tracked.
Does this affect scholarships?No. Elevate is a voluntary co-curricular program. It does not affect financial aid, scholarships, or academic standing.
Do CMC events count?Yes. Stuart CMC career coaching, the Stuart Career Fair, Career Pathway Presentations, and all CMC events count toward Elevate completion. Students do not have to choose between CMC and Career Services.
Can we create more badges?Absolutely. Tell us what competency you want to validate, define what a student needs to do to earn it, and we can have a new badge designed and live within a week.
What about the Stuart resume template?VMock feedback is calibrated to Stuart guidelines. If a student’s resume doesn’t follow Stuart format, they’re asked to revise before earning the badge.

Braven inside Elevate — Positioning

Elevate is the four-year framework. Braven is a one-semester accelerator that lives inside it. Braven is one of the most impactful experiences in the Elevate Year 2 catalog — not a separate or competing track. The shared comparison document (Elevate vs Braven, 14 dimensions) is the source of truth for student-facing communication and student-staff coaching.

Both programs are framed as intentional, complementary investments: Elevate gives students the four-year scaffolding and badge portfolio; Braven concentrates a high-impact semester of executive coaching, employer simulations, and cohort networking. Students should understand the value of both, and how Braven contributes to their overall Elevate progression.

Platform & Technology Roadmap

Engineering Elevate into the institutional spine for workforce + academic hybrid delivery · APIs, data, AI, and credentialing

Long-term technology direction

Per Malik’s direction: integrate APIs across Career Services, Campus Groups (312), Simplicity, Handshake, Coursera (Illinois Tech’s 200K+ non-credit learners), Canvas, AWS Academy, and the Registrar; explore Talon as the long-term backend for the Elevate platform; engineer the workforce + academic hybrid product with engineering and other partners.

3
AI Buckets (Ops · Innovation · Student)
200K+
Non-credit Learners (Coursera)

US “Some College, No Credential” (NSC, Jan 2024)
Adults who enrolled but never completed a credential — not all adults without a degree
1.1M

Days — AWS Transcript Processing

Six Architectural Pillars

1. API Integration Layer

Connect Campus Groups (312), Simplicity, Handshake, Canvas, Coursera, AWS Academy, Salesforce, the Registrar SIS, and Career Services tooling (VMock, Quinncia, Chiron). Single source of truth for badge issuance, activity completion, and student records.

2. Talon Backend (long-term)

Evaluate Talon as the long-term backend for the Elevate platform — identity, badge issuance, evidence storage, role-gated views, and FERPA-compliant data flows. Scoping work in progress with the new India development team being stood up to scale beyond the current single-architect build.

3. Verifiable Credentials & Open Badges 3.0

Machine-readable, employer-verifiable credentials. Aligned with MIT Digital Credentials Consortium research and the broader move to portable, lifelong credentials.

4. AI — Three Buckets

Operational efficiency (e.g., AWS reduced transcript processing from 35 days to under 2). Innovation in curriculum and assessment. Student centricity — AI TAs, Chiron avatars, AI badge recommendations.

5. Real-Time Metrics & Reporting

Replace monthly snapshot updates with live data feeds. Every metric on this dashboard is wired with data-source attributes for one-swap real-time integration.

6. Engineering & Corporate Partnership

Built with Armour engineering students and faculty; corporate partnership channels through AWS, Microsoft (60 Accenture-donated headsets), and ongoing recruiting partners. Aspires to a national replication model for other institutions.

Strategic Vision — Next Phase (May 2026)

Brand & Lifecycle Restructuring

Brand positioning across pre-college, undergraduate, graduate, alumni, and the Mumbai campus. Marketing-led with Chelsea (lead) plus Jim and Amanda.

Talent Platform Integration

Illinois Tech Talent platform — an institution-only AI-driven employer-matching alternative to Handshake. Leadership-led; Norma at CLI in conversation with Kaplan. Elevate badge metadata, skills attribution, and credentialing standards must align from day one.

Pearson Assessment Integration

Pearson is the technical mechanism for accreditation-ready validation of the new skills transcripts (soft + hard). Soft skills are Elevate’s current strength; hard skills layer in via Pearson.

India Development Team

Scoping a development team in India to scale the technical build beyond the current single-architect (Fred) infrastructure. Maps to Talon backend, real-time data, and platform expansion.

Skills Mobility Framework

Active conversation with the Gates Foundation on skills mobility. Elevate’s badge architecture is positioned to feed institutional skills mobility data nationally.

Faculty Innovation Fund

Per August 2025 directive: faculty involvement at $1,500–$25,000 per faculty-created experience, funded by a Trustee. Goal: 50 faculty experiences per academic year.

Tech Debt & Known Limitations

  • Single-architect build. Current dashboard maintained by Dr. Freddrick Logan as the sole developer. India dev team scoping is the critical-path mitigation.
  • Static export. Dashboard is currently static HTML — data values are hand-updated until API bridges land in Phase 1. data-source attributes are pre-wired for the swap.
  • FERPA boundary is policy, not platform. No raw PII in static export; role-gated views require Phase 1 SSO before they go live in the dashboard.
  • Credly footprint exceeds plan. CLI hybrid migration (see CLI page) addresses this; Open Badges 3.0 + DCC alignment is the long-range fix.
  • No real-time analytics yet. Monthly Metrics + Visualizations pages reflect manual data refresh on the 5th of each month until Phase 1 ships.

Future Plans & University Restructuring Alignment

How Elevate aligns with Illinois Tech’s university transformation · New structure announced in waves through Fall 2026

Why this matters for Elevate. The university transformation is the institutional context for everything Elevate is building. As leadership reorganizes colleges, modernizes curriculum, expands access, doubles down on online and global, and rebalances workload, Elevate is positioned to be the student-facing manifestation of the new Illinois Tech — the workforce engine, the lifelong-learning entry point, and the corporate partnership platform.

Five Transformation Pillars — and where Elevate plugs in

1. Reorganize the Colleges

Break down rigid departmental silos. Elevate alignment: badge framework already crosses school lines (Armour, Stuart, Lewis, Computing, Design, Architecture); the workforce + academic hybrid layer can absorb the new structure as it lands.

2. Tech-Infused Curriculum

Modernize how we teach. Elevate alignment: AWS Academy scaling, AI workshops, Chiron AI avatars in development, AI-powered badge recommendations, real-time data integration.

3. Expanding Access — STEM & Lifelong Learning

37.6M Americans (1.1M Illinois) classified by the National Student Clearinghouse as “Some College, No Credential” — adults who enrolled in college but never completed a credential, distinct from the broader population of Americans without college degrees. Elevate alignment: Transfer Cohort Bridge, Pre-College badges, the alumni badge network, and Coursera’s 200K+ non-credit learners are direct deliverables here.

4. Doubling Down on Online & Global — International Positioning

Illinois Tech is one of the most internationally diverse private research universities in the U.S., with students from 100+ countries and a long-standing international mission tracing back to its 1890 founding. The Elevate platform is being built to scale beyond Chicago: W3C-compliant verifiable credentials (Open Badges 3.0) travel across borders so international students — on-campus, online, and post-graduation — carry portable proof of skill into any labor market worldwide. Elevate alignment: Coursera + Risepoint online channels, Virtual Internships (14,000+ host network reaching international participants), Globe Trotter and Global Citizen badges, Study Away, language and intercultural reflection artifacts, AAC&U Diversity & Global Learning anchors, and an international alumni badge network that turns every Illinois Tech graduate into a node in a credentialed global community.

5. Sustainable Faculty/Staff Workload

Streamlining and rightsizing operations. Elevate alignment: automate badge issuance, stacked-badge triggers, real-time dashboards, AI-assisted advising — reduce manual review burden on coaches and faculty.

Crosscutting: Corporate & Federal Funding

Workforce-development certificates aligned with new federal funding scheme; AWS, Microsoft, Accenture corporate channels. Elevate alignment: credit-bearing & non-credit certificate stacks, employer badge dashboard, AWS Academy.

WindowUniversity Transformation MilestoneElevate Companion Action

Elevate’s Role in the Restructured Illinois Tech

Cross-school connector

Badge framework already crosses Armour / Stuart / Lewis / Computing / Design / Architecture. As college boundaries shift, Elevate remains the consistent skill substrate.

Lifelong-learning entry point

Pre-College → Transfer Bridge → Y1–Y4 → Alumni badge network → Coursera Career Academy. One credentialed pipeline serves the entire learner lifecycle.

Workforce-employer interface

Employer Badge Dashboard + Talent platform position Illinois Tech as a workforce-development asset for the corporate & federal funding pipeline cited in the transformation thesis.

Faculty workload-reduction lever

Automated badge issuance + Co-Curricular Plan + AI-assisted advising reduce manual review burden so faculty focus on research, teaching, and student mentorship.

Brand-narrative anchor

The Elevate Guarantee makes the transformation tangible to admitted students and parents — "You, Elevated" becomes a measurable, evidence-backed commitment.

Data & analytics backbone

53-page executive dashboard + Talon backend roadmap give senior leadership the real-time visibility required to make confident decisions during a multi-year transformation.

Risks & Mitigations

RiskLikelihood / ImpactMitigation
College boundary shifts invalidate badge mappingsMedium / MediumMap badges to skills + frameworks (NACE / P21 / WEF), not to specific colleges; revisit Crosswalk quarterly during restructuring.
Single-FTE delivery model becomes unsustainable at scaleHigh / HighRecommend 1 additional FTE (see Budget); automate stacked-badge triggers; cross-train Elevate Success Coaches.
Vendor lock-in on 312 / Credly / TalonMedium / HighAdopt DCC alignment as future-proof credentialing layer; Open Badges 3.0 as portable interchange format.
University leadership shift mid-transformationMedium / MediumDocument everything (this dashboard is the operating archive); maintain executive-readable narrative continuity.
Partner attrition (e.g., Architecture stall)Medium / MediumSenior-leadership re-engagement protocols (see Architecture page); 7-touchpoint per-semester ceiling; documented escalation paths through BJ + Mallik.

Assessments

Required & optional activity submission forms · Pre-College through Year 4 (pilot) · FERPA-safe (no PII stored on server) · framework-anchored item bank · cross-activity form scaffolding through 2027.

Methodology — How Elevate Builds Its Assessment Item Bank

Elevate’s assessment strategy is built on a single premise: every required activity, every elective, and every Elevate Success Coach check-in should produce a small, comparable, framework-anchored evidence signal. To make this scalable, Career Services & Experiential Learning is systematically analyzing each of the grounding frameworks — NACE Career Readiness, P21 21st Century Skills, WEF Future of Jobs, CASEL SEL, AAC&U HIPs, O*NET, U.S. DOE Employability Skills, Kolb Experiential Learning, and the Coursera 2026 Job Skills Report — and extracting the items most appropriate for an undergraduate, employer-facing context.

Items selected for the Elevate bank meet four criteria: (1) construct validity against the source framework; (2) readability at the typical undergraduate level; (3) brevity appropriate to a 10–15-minute coach check-in or activity form; and (4) operational alignment with a downstream badge or activity. Items are then re-purposed across the activity catalog as scaffolding for the year-specific forms below — Pre-College through Year 4 plus the Transfer Bridge — so that the same construct shows up in multiple places along a student’s four-year arc and produces a measurable longitudinal signal.

In parallel, the team is reviewing third-party assessment catalogs — including Pearson Assessments — to evaluate the career, vocational interest, leadership-style, and workplace-readiness instruments that could complement the Elevate item bank. Clinical, diagnostic, and mental-health instruments are explicitly out of scope for Elevate; these belong to the Student Health and Wellness team and are not appropriate for a career-readiness program. The catalog scan focuses exclusively on career-development, professional-skills, learning-style, and workplace-readiness instruments.

Cross-Activity Form Scaffolding — How Items Travel

A single well-designed framework item gets reused multiple times across a student’s journey — producing a longitudinal record of growth, not a one-off snapshot. The table below shows representative reuse paths.

Framework Item OriginConstruct MeasuredReuse Across Elevate Activities
NACE 8 Career Readiness CompetenciesCommunication, Critical Thinking, Teamwork, Professionalism, Technology, Leadership, Career & Self-Development, Equity & InclusionSelf-Assessment (baseline + semester re-take) → Co-Curricular Plan → Coach Check-In → Workshop pre/post → Capstone → Senior Exit Survey
WEF Future of Jobs 2025/2030Analytical thinking, AI & Big Data, Tech Literacy, Active Learning, Resilience, Curiosity, EmpathyAWS Academy pre/post → AI Workshop 4 self-assessment → Tech+ Specialist reflection → Industry Research Deep Dive
CASEL Social-Emotional LearningSelf-Awareness, Self-Management, Social Awareness, Relationship Skills, Responsible Decision-MakingElevate Success Coach Check-In → Mentorship Reflection → ELM Leadership → Transfer Activity 2 & 9
P21 4 CsCritical Thinking, Communication, Collaboration, CreativityIPRO Innovation Day rubric → Case Competition reflection → Hackathon submission → Capstone
AAC&U High-Impact PracticesFirst-year experience, learning community, internship, service learning, capstone quality indicatorsFYE Coach Check-In → Student Org Reflection → Internship Approval Form → Service Day Reflection
O*NET / U.S. DOE EmployabilityApplied academic skills, workplace readiness, occupational skillsPre-College FOCUS2 alignment → Resume Development → Co-op Approval → Senior Exit
Kolb Experiential LearningConcrete Experience → Reflective Observation → Abstract Conceptualization → Active ExperimentationEvery reflection prompt across the catalog (300–500 words) is anchored to one Kolb stage.
Coursera 2026 Job Skills ReportGenAI literacy, Data Analytics, Cybersecurity, Cloud, Human-Centered CommunicationAI Skills mini-assessment (4 items, integrated into the Framework Self-Assessment above)

How submission works. Each form saves your draft to your own browser (localStorage) so you can return to it later. When you click Submit, the form generates a printable summary and a pre-filled email to your Elevate Success Coach — nothing is uploaded to a server from this static dashboard. This protects student privacy and complies with FERPA. The official record is created when the email is sent or the printed summary is uploaded to Campus Groups (312).

Framework-Based Self-Assessment

Before filling out a year-specific form, rate yourself against the 8 NACE Career Readiness Competencies and the 4 employer-prioritized AI skills (NACE 2026 Spring Update, Coursera Job Skills 2026). Items are pulled directly from validated framework instruments — your responses stay in your browser only and become a baseline you can re-take each semester. Coaches use these to build your Co-Curricular Plan and Flight Plan.

Scale: 1 = Not at all proficient · 2 = Not very · 3 = Somewhat · 4 = Very · 5 = Extremely (mirrors NACE 2026 Job Outlook proficiency scale)

8 NACE Career Readiness Competencies
AI Skills (NACE 2026 + Coursera 2026)
Reflection prompts (CAR-Method ready)

Sources: NACE Career Readiness Competencies (2024); NACE Job Outlook 2026 Spring Update (n=185, Feb–Mar 2026); Coursera 2026 Job Skills Report. Items adapted from public NACE competency definitions and Coursera 2026 priority skill rankings.

Submit completion evidence for the 6 required Year 1 activities and any optional activities you have completed. Two optional activities required to advance.

Year 2 · Engagement

Submit completion evidence for the 7 required Year 2 activities plus any optional activities. 10 hours of engagement experience expected; format flexible (in-person/virtual, sync/async).

Required (7)

Year 3 · Accountability

40+ hours of supervised professional experience expected. Submit your professional placement details and reflection on accountability outcomes.

Required — check all completed

PILOT

Year 4 · Upskilling (Pilot)

Pilot year. 8 applications/month target. Final upskilling, job-search execution, and offer-evaluation activities. Reflection focus: progress toward the employment guarantee.

Required (Pilot)
Optional

Transfer Cohort Bridge (One-Year)

Submit completion evidence for the 9 base activities. Activity 9 triggers the Transition Placement Form — assigning you to Y1, Y2, Y3, or Y4.

9 Base Activities
Bridge-year Electives (carry forward into placement cohort)

All form data is stored only in your local browser (localStorage). Submitting generates a printable summary or a pre-filled email; no student data leaves this dashboard until you choose to send the email or print and upload the summary to Campus Groups (312).

Selected Assessments Under Review — By Framework

A non-exhaustive selection of validated, publicly available instruments Career Services is reviewing as source material for the Elevate item bank. Items drawn from these instruments are paraphrased, shortened, and re-anchored to a specific Elevate activity or workshop before deployment — we do not administer copyrighted, proprietary, or clinical instruments to students within the Elevate dashboard.

FrameworkRepresentative Assessment / InstrumentUseful For
NACE Career ReadinessNACE 8-Competency Self-Assessment (importance + proficiency scale, mirrored in NACE Job Outlook)Year 1 baseline; Year 2 mid-program re-take; Senior exit gap analysis
WEF Future of JobsWEF Reskilling Revolution Top-Skill Self-Check (15 priority skills for 2030)AI Workshop 4 pre/post; AWS Academy enrollment intake; Tech+ Specialist
P21 4 CsP21 4 Cs Self-Report (Critical Thinking, Communication, Collaboration, Creativity)IPRO Innovation Day rubric; Hackathon reflection
CASEL SELCASEL 5-Competency Self-Awareness short-form (career-adapted)Elevate Success Coach check-ins; FYE first-year reflection; ELM Leadership
AAC&U HIPsAAC&U VALUE Rubrics — Civic Engagement, Integrative Learning, Teamwork (open-license)Capstone rubric; Service Day reflection; Internship final report
O*NET / U.S. DOLO*NET Interest Profiler (RIASEC); O*NET Work Importance Locator (free, public domain)Pre-College FOCUS2 cross-walk; Career Exploration Workshop 13; major-decision support
U.S. DOE Employability SkillsDOE Employability Skills Framework Self-Check (applied academic, workplace readiness)Resume Development; Co-op Approval; Senior Exit
Kolb Experiential LearningKolb Learning Style Inventory (KLSI) constructs (CE / RO / AC / AE)Reflection-prompt scaffolding across every activity (300–500-word reflection rule)
Coursera Job Skills 2026Coursera priority-skill self-check (GenAI literacy, Data Analytics, Cybersecurity, Cloud, Communication)Workshop 4 (AI); AI Practitioner badge intake; AWS AI Foundations
Transfer Bridge (Laanan)Laanan-Transfer Students’ Questionnaire (L-TSQ; Moser, 2012)Transfer Cohort Activity 2 pre-check; Activity 9 post-check (validated longitudinal instrument)

Pearson Assessments Catalog — Exploration Scope

Career Services is reviewing the Pearson Assessments catalog for instruments that could supplement the Elevate framework item bank. Pearson publishes a large library of psychometrically validated instruments — the specific instruments under evaluation for Elevate are limited to career, vocational, learning-style, leadership, and workplace-readiness categories.

Out-of-scope (explicitly excluded). Pearson’s clinical and diagnostic catalog — psychological, neuropsychological, behavioral health, psychiatric, mood, substance use, autism / ADHD diagnostic, and any other mental-health screening or diagnostic instruments — is not appropriate for the Elevate dashboard or for career-services workflows. These instruments belong to qualified clinicians under the Student Health and Wellness team, with separate FERPA + HIPAA governance. Elevate will not request, store, administer, or interpret any clinical instrument.

Career, Vocational & Workplace-Readiness Instruments Under Review

CategoryRepresentative Pearson InstrumentPotential Elevate Use
Vocational InterestStrong Interest Inventory® (career interest themes, RIASEC, occupational scales)Pre-College FOCUS2 complement; Year 1 Coach Check-In; Career Pivoting Workshop 13
Career MaturityCareer Maturity Inventory (career-decision readiness; attitude and competence subscales)Pre-College and Year 1 baseline; Junior-year career-decision checkpoint
Personality (Workplace)Myers-Briggs Type Indicator® (MBTI®); Workplace Big Five Profile (work-related personality factors)ELM Leadership self-awareness module; Personal Branding Workshop 9; Team formation
Leadership StyleLeadership Practices Inventory (LPI®); Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ)Leadership Academy (6 workshops); Student Org Leadership reflection; Stuart C-Suite pathway
Emotional IntelligenceEQ-i 2.0® / EQ 360® (workplace emotional-intelligence framework)Elevate Success Coach prep; Mentorship reflection; Y3/Y4 internship readiness
Workplace SkillsWatson-Glaser™ Critical Thinking Appraisal; Raven’s Progressive Matrices (cognitive aptitude)Critical Thinking Workshop 2 (CAR); Industry Research Deep Dive baseline
Learning StrategiesLASSI (Learning and Study Strategies Inventory); Motivated Strategies for Learning QuestionnairePre-College + Year 1 academic-readiness; Transfer Bridge Activity 2; Workshop 1
Cross-Cultural CompetencyIntercultural Development Inventory (IDI®); Cultural Intelligence Scale (CQ)Globe Trotter / Study Away pre/post; Service Learning Abroad reflection; Stuart Global Business Navigator

Governance & Next Steps

  • Licensing review. Career Services will work with the Office of General Counsel and Pearson’s institutional licensing team to confirm cost, qualification level (A/B/C), administration / interpretation requirements, and any data-residency or storage obligations before piloting any third-party instrument.
  • FERPA & data minimization. Any third-party instrument adopted for Elevate must preserve the same privacy posture as the existing dashboard: aggregate reporting, no PII stored server-side, student-controlled export. Instruments that require server-side storage of identifiable responses will be administered through institutional systems (not this dashboard) under the existing FERPA review.
  • Clinical instruments excluded. Career Services does not have the qualified personnel to administer or interpret clinical instruments and explicitly does not seek that scope. Students presenting with mental-health concerns are referred to the Student Health and Wellness team through standard referral protocols.
  • Pilot order. Items already in the public domain (NACE 8, WEF Top Skills, P21 4 Cs, CASEL 5, AAC&U VALUE, O*NET, DOE Employability, Kolb LSI public constructs) are integrated first. Third-party instruments (Strong Interest, MBTI, LPI, EQ-i 2.0, Watson-Glaser, LASSI, IDI) are evaluated for pilot only after licensing review — targeted for the Summer 2026 build-out window.
  • Outcome. Every Elevate student leaves with a longitudinal, framework-anchored evidence record that maps cleanly to NACE 8 / WEF 2030 / employer-verifiable competencies — the durable artifact behind the employment guarantee.

Pearson Assessments product names (Strong Interest Inventory, MBTI, Watson-Glaser, EQ-i 2.0, Leadership Practices Inventory, Intercultural Development Inventory, etc.) are trademarks of their respective owners. Inclusion in this exploration scope does not constitute endorsement, licensing, or adoption. See pearsonassessments.com for the full catalog. Career Services & Experiential Learning will publish a final shortlist after the Summer 2026 licensing & FERPA review window.

Co-Curricular Plan Data

Executive summary of Elevate Success Coach check-in submissions, student goal-setting patterns, and coaching engagement across both cohorts · all student data FERPA-compliant (aggregated, no individual identifiers).

Engagement Rate (122 of 398 enrolled)

The Co-Curricular Plan is the student-authored articulation of their Elevate trajectory: goals, target activities, badge milestones, and timelines. Plans are co-developed in the first Elevate Success Coach 1:1 and revisited each term. SMART-format goals are tied directly to badge progression so progress is measurable against the framework. Plans serve as the connective tissue between the catalog (80+ activities) and the badge system (73 active types).

Year 1 Executive Summary

Year 1 Co-Curricular Plans: 97 submissions — representing 35.1% of 276 enrolled Year 1 students at the time of the 312 admin export. Peak engagement occurred in October–November 2025 during the fall semester onboarding period, with a secondary push through March–May 2026. Approximately 91% of check-ins were first-time meetings with a success coach, indicating strong initial outreach with growing repeat engagement. Five success coaches contributed historically; three currently active: Sai, Angel, Adiba (Barbie and Indrani transitioned).

How to read “Submissions”: A submission is a single form submission, not a unique student. Students can check in (and submit other forms) multiple times. For example, the 97 Check-In submissions are submitted by 95 unique Y1 students (per the May 12 tracker) — the small difference reflects students who logged more than one check-in.

Year 2 Executive Summary

Year 2 Co-Curricular Plans: 25 submissions — representing 20.8% of 120 Year 2 students enrolled at the time of the 312 admin export (108 per May 12 tracker after subsequent attrition). Year 2 engagement lags behind Year 1 by roughly 14 percentage points, reflecting the sophomore-year transition and competing academic demands. Approximately 96% of check-ins were first-time meetings, indicating the sophomore cohort has had minimal prior coaching interaction and that targeted outreach is the highest-leverage intervention for this group. As with Y1, submission counts are form submissions, not unique students — the same student can submit multiple times.

Co-Curricular Plan Word Cloud (FERPA-aggregated theme frequency)

time management job shadowing workshops volunteering internships research engineering leadership networking cybersecurity balancing procrastination F1 / motorsports game design commuting accountability mechanical resume GPA LinkedIn robotics architecture career fairs motivation confidence certifications computer AI electrical hackathons

Key Patterns from Student Co-Curricular Plans

Dominant Challenge: Time Management

56% of students (54 of 96) identified time management as their primary challenge. Commuter students and student-athletes reported compounding difficulties. Several students cited procrastination (12%) and balancing competing demands (18%) as related concerns.

Experience Gap: Job Shadowing Most Desired

56% of students want job shadowing opportunities, followed by workshops (50%), volunteering (53%), internships (32%), and research (29%). This suggests students want structured, low-barrier exposure to professional environments before committing to internships.

Engineering Dominance with Niche Interests

Engineering majors comprise approximately 62% of check-ins. Notable niche interests include F1 / motorsports, game design, neuroscience / bioinformatics, urban planning, and defense engineering. Several students referenced studying abroad (France).

First-Generation & Undecided Signals

Five students are undeclared / exploratory. Several expressed uncertainty about their major choice; one student explicitly lost interest in CS and wants to switch. Multiple students mentioned being first to navigate professional development in their families.

Student Reflection Themes (FERPA-Compliant Aggregation)

Analysis of Year 1 and Year 2 experience reflection submissions reveals key learning themes. No individual student data is disclosed below.

Year 1 Experience Types

Cultural & Arts Experiences. Art museum visits, theater performances, gallery exhibitions. Students reported growth in critical observation, analytical thinking, and appreciation for diverse perspectives.

Technical Projects. Fullstack development (TypeScript, HTML, CSS, Java), financial application building, GitHub collaboration. Students cited adaptability, time management, and technical skill acquisition.

Athletics & Team Activities. Varsity sports participation driving collaboration skills, leadership development, and time management under competing demands.

Mentorship & Professional. Meetings with industry professionals and alumni, resume development, construction document review. Students gained confidence in career direction and professional communication.

Top Skills Identified by Students

Time Management
6
Critical Thinking
5
Communication
5
Teamwork / Collab.
4
Adaptability
4
Technical Skills
3
Leadership
3
Active Listening
2

Key Insights & Recommendations

1. Strong Initial Engagement, Low Repeat. Approximately 92% of check-ins are first-time meetings, indicating the program successfully reaches students for initial contact but has not yet established regular coaching rhythms. Consider implementing structured follow-up cadences (mid-semester and end-of-semester touchpoints).

2. Year 2 Engagement Gap. Year 2 engagement (20.8%) trails Year 1 (35.1%) by approximately 14 percentage points. The sophomore cohort may benefit from targeted outreach campaigns and integration with academic advising to boost participation.

3. Coach Capacity Imbalance. In Year 1, caseloads now range from 1 to 28 students per coach. Rebalancing assignments or implementing a tiered coaching model could improve consistency and scalability.

4. Seasonal Pattern. October–November represents the peak engagement window (approximately 59% of Year 1 submissions); the March–May spring push added 24 additional plans. Spring semester programming should plan for a second engagement push with targeted incentives.

Reading the numbers. Year 1 Coach Check-In is the strongest signal of engagement at 14.5%; Year 2 Coach Check-In follows at 4.24%. Required activities with significant time commitments (Mentorship, Chicago Adventures, Pro Dev Workshop, Leadership Academy) sit in the 0.9–3.1% band — the natural targets for the Summer 2026 update push. Optional activity completion (1.1–4%) is the lever where the 2026 form refresh, framework-based self-assessment, and tighter Elevate Success Coach prompts can move the needle most.

FERPA. Only aggregated counts and percentages appear here. Raw student lists from the platform exports are not stored in the dashboard source.

Elevate Success Coach Operations

Operational model for Elevate Success Coaches — caseload management, meeting frameworks, intervention strategies, training, and student satisfaction metrics · May 2026 cohort transition.

3
Currently Active Coaches
Sai · Angel · Adiba (Barbie & Indrani transitioned)
~55
Avg Caseload · Active Students per Coach
122
Total Check-Ins (97 Y1 + 25 Y2)
~92%
First-Time Rate

May 2026 Cohort Transition

Sai (highest-volume Elevate Success Coach, 40 combined check-ins across Y1 + Y2) and Yogesh (Cohort Manager — platform support & outreach) are graduating in May 2026. Their caseloads will transition to the incoming 2026–27 team in June. The Spring 2026 hiring pipeline has yielded confirmed offers and an active candidate pool to backfill and expand capacity.

Target post-transition team: 2 Cohort Managers + 4–5 Elevate Success Coaches, with an institutional commitment to reduce average caseload from ~55 to ~35–40 students per coach.

Coaching Model Overview

Meeting Framework

  • Initial Check-In (30 min). Co-Curricular Plan development, career goal alignment, Elevate overview, badge pathway selection.
  • Follow-Up Sessions (20 min). Progress review, activity documentation support, barrier troubleshooting, resource referrals.
  • Milestone Meetings (30 min). End-of-semester reflection, badge portfolio review, next-semester planning.
  • Scheduling. Handshake appointment system with drop-in hours; CMC appointments count toward Elevate for Stuart students.

Intervention Types

  • Proactive Outreach. Email / text campaigns to students who have not completed key milestones by target dates.
  • Reactive Support. Responding to student requests for help with activities, platform navigation, or career questions.
  • Warm Referrals. Connecting students to Career Services advisors, faculty mentors, campus resources, or external opportunities.
  • Peer Coaching. Trained student coaches provide peer-to-peer support for activity completion and platform help.

Incoming Elevate Team — 2026–27 Hiring Pipeline

Spring 2026 hiring drew 53 applicants across both roles. Below: confirmed offers and the active candidate pipeline. All hires are paid at $16.60 / hr for 10 hrs / week unless otherwise noted; graduate students and international students eligible.

Confirmed Hires (Start: Week of June 1, 2026)

NameRoleProgram & LevelKey Strength

FWS poolPlatform Support / Outreach / EventsFederal Work-Study (undergrad)Awaiting interview-scheduling approval from BJ; will support proactive outreach and event programming.

Training & Onboarding

Coach Training Program

  • Week 1–2. Elevate program overview, badge system, 312 platform navigation, FERPA training.
  • Week 3–4. Motivational interviewing basics, active listening, cultural competency, scenario practice.
  • Ongoing. Weekly team meetings (Mondays), bi-weekly 1:1 supervision with Dr. Freddrick Logan, monthly professional development.
  • Shadowing. New coaches shadow 3 outgoing-coach (Sai / Yogesh) check-ins during May transition window.

Summer / Fall 2026 Hiring

  • 3 confirmed offers (Danish, Rabiraj, Deborah) start week of June 1, 2026.
  • 4–6 additional student staff in hiring pipeline for Spring / Fall 2026.
  • Positions open to undergraduate and graduate students, including international students.
  • Roles: Elevate Success Coach, Cohort Manager, Platform Support Specialist, Outreach Coordinator, Events & Programming Assistant.

Standard Coach Activities

  • First-year self-assessment review (NACE 2026 + Coursera 2026 framework alignment)
  • Co-curricular plan development (97 Y1 + 25 Y2 active plans on file)
  • SMART goal-setting tied to badge progression (5 foundational tracks × 5 tiers)
  • Progress monitoring against the year-specific checklist (8 Y1 + 9 Y2 items)
  • Connection to additional campus resources (CMC, Galvin Library, Stuart certificate programs, AWS Academy, Kaplan Institute)
  • Meeting documentation in 312 (FERPA-compliant aggregate reporting)
  • Outreach campaigns to non-engaged students (proactive nudges before milestone deadlines)
  • Stuart CMC event credit verification (per Curt A. October 2025 partnership protocol)

Key Insights & Next Steps

1. High first-time rate (~92%). Most students are meeting coaches for the first time. This is a growth opportunity for repeat engagement and deeper relationship building — targeting structured mid-semester and end-of-semester follow-up cadences.

2. Year 2 engagement gap. 20.8% co-curricular plan completion vs. 35.1% for Year 1. Targeted sophomore outreach campaigns planned for Fall 2026, integrated with academic advising.

3. Caseload optimization. With 4–6 new hires landing for Fall 2026, target caseload drops from ~55 to ~35–40 students per coach, enabling more proactive outreach and follow-up capacity.

4. Transition continuity. Sai and Yogesh wrap caseloads by May 30, 2026; their students receive a written handoff note and a warm intro to their new coach during the first June check-in. No active case will go untouched in the transition window.

312 Platform Analytics (CampusGroups)

Usage data, form completion rates, and platform engagement metrics for the 312 CampusGroups system that powers Elevate’s badge tracking, activity submissions, and student communications · branded internally as 312 after Chicago’s area code.

51+
Active Forms (25 Y1 + 26 Y2)
450+
Total Submissions (all form types combined)
1,152
Group Members (611 Y1 + 541 Y2)
398
T&C Signed (290 Y1 + 108 Y2)
17
Active Checklist Items (8 Y1 + 9 Y2)
73
Active Badges Tracked
45.2%
Y1 Conversion to Enrolled (276 of 611)
22.2%
Y2 Conversion to Enrolled (120 of 541)

Platform Architecture

312 CampusGroups (Primary)

  • Function. Badge tracking, activity form submissions, T&C signatures, co-curricular plan management, group communications.
  • Forms engine. Custom forms for each activity type with approval workflows (coach review → manager approval).
  • Reporting. Real-time dashboards, CSV export, badge progress tracking per student.
  • Vendor. Built by Ready Education on an institution-wide subscription. Parent-child group structure with sequential checklists. Year 1 (276 enrolled) and Year 2 (120 enrolled, 99% commuters) are the primary cohort groups.

Integrated Platforms

  • Handshake. Appointment scheduling, employer connections, career fair registration, job & internship postings.
  • uConnect (Elevate Portal at elevate.iit.edu). Public-facing hub, curriculum-based pathways, blog content, resource library.
  • Credly. Digital badge issuance and verification for external-facing credentials.
  • VMock. AI resume review with Stuart-specific template calibration.
  • AWS Academy / Coursera Career Academy. Cloud and lifelong-learning content cross-walked to Elevate badges.

Form Completion Analytics

Migration & Long-Term Architecture

Simplicity platform evaluation. A potential alternative to 312 is under evaluation; it would offer automated badge issuance triggers, improved analytics, and tighter Handshake integration. See Partnerships → Platform & Technology Roadmap for the full scoping document.

Talon backend (planned). The long-term student-portfolio backend (Open Badges 3.0, verifiable credentials, employer-verifier endpoint) is scoped against Talon. This sits behind both 312 and uConnect.

FERPA. All platform analytics shown in this dashboard are aggregated. Per-student rosters live inside 312 and the institutional SIS — never in this dashboard’s source code.

2026 UI/UX Launch

Major platform update to the Elevate student experience on elevate.iit.edu — restructured experience categories, improved navigation, and community-based filtering for Required and Optional Elevate Experiences.

elevate.iit.edu
Public Portal (uConnect)
312.iit.edu
Engagement Platform
Live
Current Status
Fall 2026
Full Launch Target

Elevate Portal Redesign

The 2026 UI update introduces community-based experience filtering, separating Required and Optional Elevate Experiences into distinct navigational paths. Students filter by cohort (Pre-College, First-Year, Sophomore, Junior, Transfer, Commuter) to find relevant activities instantly. The student-facing public portal (uConnect at elevate.iit.edu) is the primary surface; the engagement platform (312) is the action layer for badge tracking and form submissions.

New Navigation Structure

Required Elevate Experiences

elevate.iit.edu/channels/required-elevate-experiences/

Community-filtered view of all mandatory activities. Students select their cohort to see only relevant requirements — the central place students go to confirm “what do I have to do this year?”

CommunityRequired Activities
Pre-CollegeDevUp Scholars activities + Financial Literacy
First-Year6 required — Coach Check-in, Resume, Mentorship, Student Org, Experience, Chicago Adventures
Sophomore7 required — Handshake, LinkedIn, Coach Check-in, Resume Review, Experience, Workshop, Leadership Academy
Junior7 required — mirrors Sophomore with advanced expectations

Optional Elevate Experiences Primary Hub Focus

elevate.iit.edu/channels/optional-elevate-experiences/

Elective and optional activities filtered by community — this is where the Elevate Careers Portal hub lives and where the bulk of student exploration happens. Includes partnership activities (AWS, Kaplan, Stuart, Braven), commuter experiences (Podcast Series, Virtual Interviews, Digital Portfolio), and specialized tracks (ELM, Globe Trotter, Industry Research). The Optional Experiences channel is the discovery engine that lets students self-author their pathway beyond the required core.

CommunityOptional Activities
Pre-CollegeStudent Org Exploration I & II, STEM Mentorship Continuation
First-YearStuart Tier 4 activities, certifications, portfolio development
SophomoreStuart Tier 4 activities, certifications, portfolio development, BRVN 200 (Braven)
TransferELM, Globe Trotter, Portfolio, Industry Research, Cultural Immersion
CommuterPodcast Series, Virtual Interviews, Certifications, AI Tools, Digital Portfolio, Virtual Events

How Optional Activities Are Presented on the Elevate Careers Portal Hub

The Optional Experiences channel is the centerpiece of the 2026 hub redesign. It is engineered to let a student answer four questions in one screen: What can I do right now? Why should I care? How long does it take? What badge do I get?

Community Filter Chips

Pre-College · First-Year · Sophomore · Junior · Transfer · Commuter. Click a chip and the page reflows to only show electives relevant to that cohort. Multiple chips combine (e.g., Transfer + Commuter).

Tier & Hours Badge on Each Card

Each activity card shows the Elevate tier (L1 Foundation → L5 Elevator), time commitment (1–5 hr, 10–20 hr, 20–40 hr, 40+ hr), and badge alignment so students can size up effort at a glance.

Format & Modality Tags

Virtual, hybrid, in-person, async, sync — surfaced as tags. Commuter students can filter to async-only in one click.

Partnership & Track Pills

AWS Academy, Kaplan Institute, Stuart School, Braven, ELM Leadership, Esports, Study Away — partnership pills connect each activity to its parent program for context.

Badge Earn Preview

Hovering or expanding a card reveals every badge the activity earns — so students see how a single elective stacks toward their C-Suite, Future-CTO, or Global Citizen progression.

Curriculum-Based & Affinity Adjacency

Career Pathways, Curriculum-Based Pathways (Global Career Accelerator, Salesforce Foundations, AWS Certifications), and Affinity Group pages (International, Transfer, Veterans, All Abilities) sit alongside Optional Experiences in the main nav — one click between exploration and enrollment.

Platform Architecture

The Elevate portal runs on the uConnect platform integrated with Illinois Tech branding. Career Pathways, Affinity Group pages (International, Transfer, Veterans, All Abilities), and Curriculum-Based Pathways (Global Career Accelerator, Salesforce Foundations, AWS Certifications) are accessible through the main navigation alongside the restructured Elevate Experiences section. uConnect strips modern CSS from custom HTML — layouts require table-based HTML for portability, which is reflected in build conventions.

Product Implementation Ideas

Concepts under active scoping for the 2026–27 platform cycle. Each is sized as a discrete product epic with owner, dependencies, and success metric.

Mobile-First Experience

Re-engineer uConnect and 312 student-facing flows for mobile. 99% of Y2 is commuter; many students engage Elevate via phone between classes.

Single Sign-On (SSO)

Illinois Tech IdP (Microsoft 365 / Azure AD) for one-click access to Elevate portal, 312, Handshake, AWS Academy, VMock.

AI Badge Recommendations

Personalized “what to do next” suggestions based on cohort, major, completed activities, and gap analysis vs. badge requirements.

Open Badges 3.0 Portability

Every earned badge is a Verifiable Credential. Students can share to LinkedIn, employer dashboards, or a portable portfolio URL.

Real-Time Progress Tracking

Live progress bar per checklist; “X of 8 items complete, on track for 2027 graduation.” Push notifications for missing items.

Coach Workspace

Coach-facing view of caseload, upcoming check-ins, students at risk, and badge issuance queue.

Optional-Experience Recommender

On the Optional Experiences hub, surface 3–5 personalized electives per student based on stated career interests + Co-Curricular Plan + remaining gaps.

Employer Verifier Endpoint

Public-facing badge verification URL where employers paste a Verifiable Credential and instantly see the activity, framework alignment, and issuance date.

Parent / Family Opt-In View

Optional parent / family dashboard (FERPA-safe, student-controlled) showing badge progression and milestone proximity. Strong recruitment signal for prospective students.

Platforms in Scope

SurfaceOwnerRefresh Scope
elevate.iit.edu (uConnect)Mary CantorChannel restructure, Required + Optional split, community filtering, mobile-first, table-HTML compliance, employer-facing pages
312.iit.edu (Campus Groups)Ready EducationChecklist UX, badge issuance queue, SSO, AI nudges, coach workspace
Elevate Dashboard (this site)Fred Logan / India dev teamExecutive analytics, API-driven data swap, real-time metrics
Student Portfolio (new)Talon backend (planned)Shareable badge portfolio URL, Open Badges 3.0, employer-verifier endpoint

Launch Plan

  • Fall 2026: Wireframes & user testing with 8–10 student staff; SSO scoping with OTS; uConnect channel content refresh; Optional Experiences hub IA finalized.
  • January 2027: Soft launch with the Y1 incoming cohort; instrument analytics; gather first-month feedback on Required + Optional split.
  • Spring 2027: Full launch to all enrolled students (612 Y1 + 542 Y2); coach workspace activated; community filtering live.
  • Fall 2027: AI badge recommender goes live on the Optional Experiences hub; first Verifiable Credential issued via Talon backend.

Pre-College Pathway Metrics

Career development that begins before classes start — high-school juniors/seniors admitted to or considering Illinois Tech.

Summer 2026
Pilot Launch
8
Pre-College Activities
FOCUS2
Career Interest Assessment

Components: Career interest assessment (FOCUS2), professional platform setup (LinkedIn, Handshake) before fall enrollment, Elevate Success Coach introduction and goal-setting, summer bridge programs, early badge-earning opportunities. The Pre-College pathway is the entry point of the end-to-end lifecycle Elevate spans pre-college through alumni engagement.

Pre-College activity catalog: Digital Literacy Foundation Building · Elevate Success Coach Connection · Virtual Career Exploration Event · Student Organization Exploration I & II · Financial Literacy for College Success · Pre-College Orientation Completion · FOCUS2 Career Interest Assessment · Pre-College LinkedIn Profile Creation.

Why a Pre-College Pathway?

Per the institutional lifecycle vision, Elevate is end-to-end — pre-college through alumni. Pre-college engagement does three things at once: (1) builds the prospective-student pipeline by giving admitted students a tangible reason to choose Illinois Tech, (2) starts career development before fall arrival so first-year students show up ready, and (3) builds early relationship equity with Elevate Success Coaches so the Y1 Coach Check-In (currently 29% completion) starts higher.

Target population: admitted students between admission decision (March–May) and matriculation (August). Approximately 1,800–2,200 admitted students annually; pilot target ~10% engagement = ~200 pre-college participants.

Eight Pre-College Activities — Detail

ActivityFormatWhat It Does
Digital Literacy Foundation BuildingAsyncBuilds baseline tech fluency before matriculation; sets up Digital Literacy L1 trajectory.
Elevate Success Coach Connection (Pre-College)Virtual 1:1First introduction to assigned coach; sets goals for fall semester.
Virtual Career Exploration EventLive virtualIndustry / employer exposure during summer; shapes Y1 Co-Curricular Plan.
Student Organization Exploration I & IIAsync / virtualFamiliarizes students with Find Your Flock options before arrival.
Financial Literacy for College SuccessAsync moduleBudgeting basics, student loans, work-study readiness.
Pre-College Orientation CompletionIn-person / virtualElevate-specific orientation segment within general university orientation.
FOCUS2 Career Interest AssessmentSelf-pacedValidated career-interest inventory (industry standard). Outputs feed first Coach Check-In agenda.
Pre-College LinkedIn Profile CreationAsyncEstablishes professional digital presence before classes start.

Summer 2026 Pilot Plan

  • May 2026: Confirm admitted-student list; coordinate with Admissions for opt-in messaging.
  • June 2026: Launch Digital Literacy + FOCUS2 + LinkedIn modules (async).
  • July 2026: Live virtual career-exploration events; first Pre-College Coach Connect 1:1s.
  • August 2026: Pre-College Orientation Completion at university New Student Welcome; pre-college badges issued.
  • Target: 200 pre-college participants — ~10% of admitted students — arriving with Co-Curricular Plan ready Day 1.

Study Away & International Experiences

Global and domestic experiential learning opportunities integrated into the Elevate framework, enabling students to earn badges and fulfill requirements through cultural immersion, international research, and study away programs.

Global Citizen
Primary Badge Track
International & Domestic
Experiences Available
40+
Countries via Study Away
