Global Citizen Badge Earners

Live Portals & Resources

  • Study Away Office — primary portal. studyaway.iit.edu/_portal/ — program search, application portal, advising appointments, scholarship database, and pre-departure forms.
  • Elevate Study Abroad channel. elevate.iit.edu/experiences/category/study-abroad/ — curated Elevate-credit-eligible study away experiences with cohort filters.
  • Global Career Accelerator. careeraccelerator.iit.edu — the structured 3-credit Tier 3 international-career pathway.
  • Office of International Affairs. Visa, OPT / CPT, and re-entry guidance for international students participating in study away.

Global Experiences for Career Readiness

Elevate recognizes that global competency is a critical career differentiator. Study away experiences count toward both required and elective Elevate activities while building cross-cultural communication, adaptability, and professional network diversity. Every program anchors to AAC&U Diversity & Global Learning (HIP), Kolb’s Concrete Experience cycle, CASEL Social Awareness, NACE Equity & Inclusion, and P21 Global Awareness.

Available Study Away Experiences

Globe Trotter — Cultural Immersion

All Years · Required-eligible

Students complete a structured cultural-immersion experience locally or abroad, documenting their engagement through reflections and portfolio evidence. Available to all cohorts as both a required and elective pathway.

Global CitizenCareer Critical Thinker

International Cultural Immersion (Elective)

All Years · Elective

Extended international experience through study abroad, international research collaboration, or service-learning programs. Students document cross-cultural professional competencies gained.

Global CitizenInnovation Catalyst

DIY Global Impact Experience

Year 1 Optional · Self-directed

Self-designed international or cross-cultural project that demonstrates initiative, cultural competency, and professional growth. Flight Plan pathway for custom global experiences.

Innovation CatalystGlobal Citizen

Global Career Accelerator

Curriculum-Based Pathway. The Global Career Accelerator at Illinois Tech (careeraccelerator.iit.edu) provides structured international career-development experiences. Students who complete the accelerator program earn Elevate credit toward their required experiential learning hours and qualify for the Global Business Navigator badge through the Stuart School partnership track.

Chicago Adventures (Required — Year 1)

While not international, Chicago Adventures serves as a domestic study away experience, immersing students in the cultural, professional, and civic landscape of Chicago. Students explore neighborhoods, museums, cultural institutions, and community organizations. FYE mentors coordinate museum free days and city-exploration events, providing natural fulfillment pathways for this requirement.

Global Business Navigator (Stuart Partnership)

40+ hour deep engagement through Stuart School of Business. Students develop international business competency through cross-cultural projects, global market analysis, and international team collaboration. Earns the Global Citizen badge, the Career Critical Thinker badge, and the exclusive Global Business Navigator partnership badge.

Available Study Away Programs

Illinois Tech’s Study Away office (studyaway.iit.edu) coordinates semester, summer, and short-term programs across 40+ countries. All study away experiences can count toward Elevate requirements and badge progression.

Program TypeDurationDestinationsElevate Badges
Semester ExchangeFull semesterEurope, Asia, Australia, South AmericaGlobal Citizen, Study Away Experience, Cultural Intelligence Leader
Summer Programs4–8 weeksMultiple global locationsGlobal Citizen, Innovation Catalyst
Faculty-Led Short-Term1–3 weeksVaries by program / yearGlobal Citizen, Collaboration Champion
Research Abroad8–12 weeksPartner institutionsResearch Scholar, Global Citizen, International Experience Practitioner
Service Learning Abroad1–4 weeksDeveloping nations / partner NGOsCommunity Impact Leader, Global Citizen, Global Community Developer
Global Career AcceleratorSemesterMultiple (via Stuart School)Global Citizen, Career Critical Thinker, Global Business Navigator
Globe Trotter Cultural ImmersionVariesLocal & internationalGlobal Citizen, Collaboration Champion

Study Away & Elevate Integration

Pre-Departure Requirements

Students complete a pre-departure orientation including goal-setting through their Co-Curricular Plan, identification of target badges, and a pre-reflection on expected learning outcomes. Elevate Success Coaches help align study away choices with career-development goals. Pre-departure forms are available at studyaway.iit.edu/_portal/.

Post-Experience Documentation

Upon return, students submit a 500-word reflection (anchored to Kolb’s Reflective Observation stage), portfolio evidence (photos, project artifacts), and updated LinkedIn / Handshake profiles. Satisfies the Elevate Experience requirement for applicable program years and counts toward the Global Citizen badge.

Framework Anchors

  • AAC&U Diversity & Global Learning (HIP) — the foundational rationale for Elevate’s study-away requirement design.
  • Kolb’s Experiential Learning Cycle — Concrete Experience → Reflective Observation → Abstract Conceptualization → Active Experimentation. The 500-word reflection requirement closes the cycle.
  • CASEL Social Awareness — cross-cultural competence, empathy, and perspective-taking developed through immersive experience.
  • NACE Equity & Inclusion — Year 2 NACE competency directly addressed.
  • P21 Global Awareness (Life & Career Skills) — 21st Century Learning framework alignment.
  • WEF Future of Jobs 2025 Cross-Cultural Competence — ranked in the top-20 skills for the 2025–2030 hiring window.

Study Away Contacts & Next Steps

Study Away Office. studyaway.iit.edu/_portal/ — application portal, program search, advising appointments, and scholarship database.

Elevate Coach. Talk to your Elevate Success Coach to align study away choice with your Co-Curricular Plan and target badge pathway. Email elevate@illinoistech.edu or contact Dr. Freddrick Logan, PhD · Experiential Learning Manager · flogan1@illinoistech.edu.

International Students. Office of International Affairs for visa, OPT / CPT, and re-entry guidance before submitting a study away application.

Mentorship Metrics

Alumni Mentorship Program participation and badge earning across cohorts.

Y1
Required Activity
Mentorship Match
Milestone Badge
CASEL
Relationship Skills Anchor

The Elevate Mentorship Program is a Year 1 required activity. Students are paired with alumni or industry mentors who guide career exploration, networking, and professional norms. Completion earns the Mentorship Match milestone badge.

Framework anchors: NACE Career & Self-Development; CASEL Relationship Skills; AAC&U Learning Communities. Pairing draws from the Illinois Tech alumni network and Career Services employer partners.

Mentorship Pathways — Multiple Tracks Recognized

Illinois Tech University Mentor (FYE)

Every first-year student is assigned a university mentor through First-Year Experience / Success (FYE). Default Elevate-recognized path. Contact Tanisha Earwin (tearwin@illinoistech.edu) to connect with your assigned mentor.

ELM — Empowerment, Leadership & Mentorship

Structured 10+ hour ELM Leadership Training certification (Y1 elective / Y2 required pathway). Badges: Leadership L2, Collaboration L2.

External Mentorship Program

Off-campus mentorship through professional associations, alumni networks, or community organizations. Requires coach pre-approval and documented meeting log.

Alumni Mentorship Match

Career Services + Alumni Office co-pairing program. Earns the Mentorship Match milestone badge plus Pro Relationship Builder. Active recruitment through the annual alumni network drive.

Peer / Near-Peer Mentorship

Upperclassmen mentor Y1 / transfer students. Tutoring leadership roles count. Mapped to ELM L2 + Leadership progression.

Faculty / Supervisor Mentorship

Coach-approved mentor relationship with a faculty member or workplace supervisor. Common for Y3 / Y4 students in research or internships.

How to Get Credit

  1. Identify your mentorship pathway (FYE default, ELM, external, alumni match, peer, or faculty).
  2. Hold at least one documented mentor meeting in the academic year.
  3. Submit proof: mentor confirmation email, meeting log, or program completion certificate.
  4. Submit reflection (200–500 words) describing what you learned and how it shapes your Co-Curricular Plan.
  5. Coach reviews and awards the Mentorship Match badge plus Pro Relationship Builder.

Mentorship Metrics & Outcomes

Y1 Req
Activity Status
1.6%
Y1 Completion (May 2026)
~4
Y1 Mentorship Completers
+40%
Program Completion Lift w/ Coaching
80K+
Living Illinois Tech Alumni
130+
Countries with Illinois Tech Alumni

Why Mentorship Matters — The Four-Year Arc

Elevate is building the most durable career-readiness asset Illinois Tech has: a four-year mentorship arc that begins on Day 1 and follows a student through their first career inflection point. The Purdue-Gallup Index (2014) established that students with a mentor who cared about them as a person are 2.2× more likely to be engaged at work after graduation; students with a mentor who encouraged them to pursue their goals and dreams are 1.9× more likely to thrive in well-being five years out. Mentorship is not an “extra” service — it is the highest-leverage intervention in the catalog.

The four-year arc, by year: Y1 First-Year Experience (FYE) mentor · Y2 ELM Leadership cohort coach · Y3 alumni mentor matched by industry · Y4 industry / employer mentor through partner companies (in development). Each year layers on top of the prior — not replaces — so by graduation a student has been seen, heard, and supported by four different perspectives.

The Mentorship Tiers — Year-by-Year

YearPrimary Mentor TierOwnerAnchor Badge
Year 1FYE University Mentor — first-year orientation, belonging, study-habit coachingTanisha Earwin (FYE) + Elevate CoachMentorship Match (Milestone)
Year 2ELM Leadership Cohort Coach + Peer / Near-Peer mentor — leadership, collaboration, project ownershipELM Program + Student AffairsLeadership L2 + Collaboration L2
Year 3Alumni Mentor matched by industry / function — field-specific guidance, network introductions, resume / portfolio feedbackCareer Services + Office of Alumni EngagementPro Relationship Builder + Industry Navigator
Year 4Industry / Employer Mentor through partner companies (Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Kaplan, Fermilab, etc.) — senior-year job search, interview prep, salary negotiationCareer Services + Partner Companies (in dev)Career Readiness L4 + Future C-Suite (Stuart pathway)

What Each Mentor Tier Delivers

FYE Mentor (Year 1)

Helps the student arrive, belong, and stabilize academically. Touchpoints: orientation, monthly check-ins, mid-semester wellness reach-out. Connects to: residence life, academic advising, Career Services intake.

ELM Leadership Coach (Year 2)

Three-tier ELM badge progression (Practitioner → Specialist → Mentor). Cohort-based leadership development across 10+ hours. Connects to: Student Organization Leadership, PM Excellence Club, Stuart C-Suite pathway.

Peer / Near-Peer Mentor

Upperclassmen mentor Y1 / Transfer students; tutoring leadership roles count. The lowest-cost, highest-belonging mentorship tier — runs in parallel with FYE.

Faculty / Supervisor Mentor

Coach-approved relationship with a faculty member or workplace supervisor. Common in Y3 / Y4 research labs, co-ops, and undergraduate research projects. High signal value for grad-school applications.

Alumni Mentor (Year 3+)

The flagship Elevate mentorship asset. Career Services + Office of Alumni Engagement match by industry, function, location, and student-stated goals. Earns Mentorship Match + Pro Relationship Builder.

Industry / Employer Mentor (Year 4)

In development. Sourced from anchor employer relationships (Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Kaplan, Microsoft, Accenture, Salesforce). Timed to the senior-year job search — the Elevate counterpart to Braven’s 12–15 week post-course professional mentor.

Global Alumni Mentorship Activation — The 80,000-Alumni Strategy

The asset. Illinois Tech has 80,000+ living alumni in 130+ countries — a network roughly 8× the size of Braven’s national fellowship base. The infrastructure exists; what’s needed is the activation layer that turns latent connections into Elevate-credit mentorship relationships at scale.

The opportunity. Every Elevate student who graduates becomes a future potential alumni mentor. If roughly 0.6–1% of living alumni were activated as Elevate mentors, that could translate to 500–800 active mentor relationships per year as a long-run aspirational ceiling — meaningfully broadening Y3 access to alumni mentorship. This is a directional target, contingent on Office of Alumni Engagement partnership, MOU sign-off, and sustained recruitment over multiple years.

10 Global Activation Ideas for Alumni Mentorship

IDEA 1

Regional Alumni Hubs — 10 City Mentor Nodes

Stand up alumni mentor hubs in Chicago, NYC, SF Bay, Seattle, Boston, Austin, DC, London, Mumbai, and Singapore. Each hub runs a quarterly virtual cohort (8–10 students × 1 alumni lead) plus an annual in-person event. Closely mirrors the Office of Alumni Engagement’s existing regional chapter structure.

IDEA 2

Industry-Function Matching Algorithm

Match by 3-dimensional fit: industry (alumnus’s sector) × function (alumnus’s role family) × student-stated career interest. Pull from LinkedIn-confirmed alumni profiles via the planned Open Badges 3.0 / Verifiable Credentials issuance pipeline.

IDEA 3

Global Time-Zone Office Hours

Weekly rotating “Ask An Alum” virtual office hours covering APAC, EMEA, and Americas time zones. Alumni in each region host 60-minute open-mic sessions; students drop in for advice without a long-term commitment. Counts as Y2 / Y3 Networking elective.

IDEA 4

Alumni Mentor Micro-Commit — 4 Hours / Year

Lower the barrier: one 60-min onboarding + four 60-min student sessions across the year = 4 hours total. Alumni who can’t commit to a yearlong mentor relationship can still participate. Recognized with an “Elevate Alumni Mentor 2026–27” badge.

IDEA 5

Alumni-Hosted Capstone Sponsors

Alumni working at hiring-mode companies sponsor a 5-week Elevate Capstone (analogous to the Braven format), bringing a real business challenge and panel-presenting the solution. Pulls alumni who would rather solve a problem than “mentor.”

IDEA 6

International Student → Alumni-Same-Country Match

For international students planning to return home post-graduation, prioritize matching with alumni currently working in their home country. Directly addresses the OPT / CPT and re-entry job-market gap. Mumbai, Bangalore, Hyderabad, Seoul, Beijing, Shanghai, Mexico City as initial focus.

IDEA 7

Affinity Mentor Networks

Stand up alumni mentor pods around shared identity / experience: First-Gen Alumni, Veterans, Working Parents, Transfer Alumni, International Alumni, Pre-Health Alumni, LGBTQ+ Alumni. Each pod operates as a quarterly cohort with a 12-week curriculum mapped to NACE + CASEL.

IDEA 8

Reverse Mentorship — Student → Alumni

Students with current AI, cybersecurity, GenAI, or Coursera 2026 priority skills mentor mid-career alumni learning those skills. Earns the student a Leadership L3 + Pro Comms L3 badge; gives alumni a reason to re-engage with their alma mater.

IDEA 9

Alumni Mentor Network × Coursera

Pair Coursera Career Academy alumni learners with current Elevate students on the same priority-skill track (GenAI, Data Analytics, Cybersecurity, Cloud). Co-learning model strengthens both groups. Counts toward the Coursera-Illinois-Tech lifelong-learning pillar.

IDEA 10

Alumni Mentor of the Year + Stories Pipeline

Annual recognition program + alumni storytelling pipeline (video + LinkedIn + Alumni Magazine). Surfaces success stories, retains existing mentors, and recruits new ones. Mirrors the Braven Fellow testimonial pipeline that powers their national network.

Aspirational Activation Roadmap — Summer 2026 through 2030

Aspirational. Targets below are intentionally directional, not operational commitments. Actual pace depends on Office of Alumni Engagement bandwidth, MOU sign-off, LinkedIn campaign uptake, and student cohort sizes through 2030. Numbers are anchors for planning, revisited annually.

WindowActivation WorkstreamAspirational Target
Summer 2026Open conversations with the Office of Alumni Engagement; scope an MOU draft; sketch an alumni mentor onboarding playbook; soft LinkedIn outreach to interested alumni.15–25 alumni interested in mentoring
Fall 2026Pilot first matches for a small Y3 sub-cohort; Chicago-area focus to start; explore feasibility of a NYC or SF satellite as alumni density allows.25–40 pilot matches
Spring 2027Refine matching approach based on pilot feedback; test affinity pods (e.g., First-Gen, International, Transfer) where alumni capacity exists; first Alumni Mentor recognition.75–100 active matches
Fall 2027If pilot signal is positive, explore international student → same-country alumni matches (top sender countries first); consider reverse-mentorship as student capacity allows.~150 active matches
20283–5 regional hubs piloting (subset of Chicago, NYC, SF, Seattle, Boston, Austin, DC, London, Mumbai, Singapore depending on alumni density and partner readiness); Capstone-sponsor alumni cohort under consideration.~250 active matches
By 2030Aspirational ceiling: up to 500+ active mentor relationships per year if the alumni activation thesis holds (~0.6% of 80,000+ living alumni). The goal is to broaden access to mentorship for Y3 students — not a guaranteed 1:1 entitlement. Mentorship becomes a durable Elevate career-readiness asset alongside coaches, partnerships, and badges.~500+ active matches

Cross-Functional Partners for Global Alumni Activation

Office of Alumni Engagement

Regional chapter network, alumni-board structure, alumni-affinity groups, alumni-magazine pipeline. The institutional partner that turns 80K alumni from a number into a mobilized cohort.

Office of International Affairs

International student visa, OPT / CPT, and re-entry guidance. Critical partner for the International Student → Same-Country Alumni Match pathway.

Office of Advancement

Donor + alumni relationship intelligence; identifies the “super-connector” alumni who can recruit other mentors at scale.

Career Services Anchor Partners

Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Kaplan, Microsoft, Accenture, Salesforce — each anchor employer is an alumni-mentor recruitment node for Y4 industry-mentor matches.

Center for Learning Innovation (CLI)

CLI’s alumni-badge-network roadmap is the technical layer for ongoing alumni-mentor recognition (badge issuance, LinkedIn verification, Open Badges 3.0).

Stuart School of Business CMC

Stuart’s Career Management Center co-pairing model is the operational playbook for Career-Services-plus-school-based mentorship matching at scale.

Quantum Computing

Chicago Quantum Exchange (CQE) Q-Ready advisory participation and badge pathway development.

CQE
Chicago Quantum Exchange
Q-Ready
Advisory Committee
In Dev
Activity Catalog

Illinois Tech participates in the Chicago Quantum Exchange Q-Ready advisory committee. Quantum-specific Elevate activities are in development, designed to position students for the emerging quantum workforce. Foundational alignment with the Chicago Quantum Exchange workforce-development initiative (2025).

Why Quantum Now

The Chicago region has become a national quantum-computing hub, anchored by the Chicago Quantum Exchange (CQE) — a partnership across the University of Chicago, Argonne National Laboratory, Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory, the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, and Northwestern. Illinois Tech is a participating institution. Foxhoven et al. (2025) categorize the quantum industry into research, software, hardware, applications, education, and policy roles — the Elevate quantum track is being designed to seed students into each.

Workforce gap: the global quantum talent pipeline is constrained; entry-level roles require fundamentals + at least one applied project. Elevate’s badge model is well-suited to formalize these competencies and translate them for employers.

Planned Activity Pathways

Quantum Foundations Workshop

Introductory primer on quantum mechanics for computing students. NACE Technology, P21 Critical Thinking. Badges: Digital Literacy L1, Critical Thinker L2.

CQE Research Symposium Attendance

Annual Chicago Quantum Summit attendance + reflection. AAC&U HIPs. Badges: Industry Navigator, Pro Comms L2.

Quantum Programming Project

Hands-on Qiskit / Cirq / PennyLane project. Y2 elective. Badges: Digital Literacy L3–L4, Innovation Catalyst.

Argonne / Fermilab Site Visit

Tour of quantum-research facilities; meet PIs and postdocs. Badges: Career Explorer, Industry Navigator.

Quantum Industry Career Path Analysis

Map quantum-industry role categories (Foxhoven 2025) to Illinois Tech majors and Elevate badges. Badges: Critical Thinker L3, Career Strategist.

Quantum-Adjacent Internship

Hosted internship with CQE partner orgs, vendor partners (IBM Quantum Network, IonQ, Rigetti). Y3+ pathway.

Roadmap & Open Questions

  • Summer 2026: Confirm CQE participation level, attend Q-Ready advisory; identify Illinois Tech faculty quantum leads.
  • Fall 2026: Pilot Quantum Foundations Workshop with first cohort (10–20 students).
  • Spring 2027: Launch Quantum Programming Project as Y2 elective; first Argonne / Fermilab site visit.
  • Open questions: Cross-school participation (Armour + Computing + Stuart math/finance); badge naming convention; whether quantum becomes a Specialized Track or sits inside Digital Literacy L4–L5.

Transfer Student Metrics

Population analytics for transfer students — the cohort served by the Transfer Cohort Bridge pilot · anticipated metrics ahead of the Fall 2026 launch.

These Are Anticipated Metrics for the Fall 2026 Bridge Launch

The figures below are pre-launch targets, not realized outcomes. The Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway is being built out and finalized over Summer 2026 for the Fall 2026 launch; the first observed metrics will land at the end of the Fall 2026 / Spring 2027 academic year, with the validated L-TSQ post-administration providing the first longitudinal data point in May 2027.

Full bridge curriculum, 9-activity sequence, summer build-out plan, frameworks cited, and cross-functional partner list: Program → Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway.

Who Are Illinois Tech’s Transfer Students?

Top feeders. City Colleges of Chicago, College of DuPage, Moraine Valley, and Triton are the four largest community-college pipelines into Illinois Tech. Together they represent the majority of incoming transfer credits each cycle.

The national research backdrop. Only 16% of community-college entrants transfer and complete a bachelor’s within six years — dropping to 9–13% for Black, Hispanic, low-income, and older students (Velasco, Fink & Jenkins, 2024). Transfer students lose, on average, ~50% of earned credits during institutional handoff (Monaghan & Attewell, 2015), and Ishitani’s longitudinal work documents a measurable “transfer shock” in GPA and engagement during the first post-transfer term.

The Elevate response. A one-year Transfer Cohort Bridge that meets transfers where they are, credits what they have already done, and never lets a transfer reach graduation without a clear path to the employment guarantee. The bridge year is identical to Y1 / Y2 in evidence rigor — not a parallel track and not a lower bar.

Eight Transfer Personas the Bridge Serves

Community College Transfer

Top feeders: City Colleges of Chicago, College of DuPage, Moraine Valley, Triton. Typical pattern: 2 years CC + 2 years Illinois Tech. Largest single persona group.

University Transfer

From another 4-year institution; usually after 1–2 years; often academic-fit or financial reasons. Prior coursework needs evaluation but minimal credit-loss risk.

Working Professional Return

Returning to complete a bachelor’s after time in workforce. Brings substantial work experience to credit via the Prior Learning & Experience Audit (Activity 3).

International Transfer

Transferring from an international institution. Often F-1 status considerations; OPT / CPT planning starts immediately. Coordinates with Office of International Affairs.

Military Veteran

Post-9/11 GI Bill recipients; ACE-recommended credits for military training; coordinates with Illinois Tech Veteran Services for benefits + transition.

Parent / Caregiver Return

Returning to college while balancing family responsibilities; benefits heavily from commuter-friendly async electives + flex-schedule coaching cadence.

Dual-Enrolled Accelerator

Strong high-school students arriving with significant transfer credit (AP, IB, dual enrollment); on track for early graduation; bridge year accelerates Elevate progression.

Late-Stage Major Change

Internal “transfer” — switching major late requires an Elevate reset. Bridge logic adapted internally; coach-routed prior-learning credit applied.

Transfer Frameworks Anchoring the Pilot

  • Laanan’s Transfer Student Capital (TSC). Transfers arrive with accumulated knowledge that predicts persistence and self-efficacy when explicitly surfaced.
  • Schlossberg’s Transition Theory (4 S’s). Situation, Self, Support, Strategies — structures the Transfer Cohort coaching cadence across Moving-In, Moving-Through, and Moving-Out phases.
  • Transfer Receptive Culture (Jain, Bernal Melendez, Herrera). Reframes transfer success as institutional responsibility, not student responsibility.
  • CCRC / Aspen Transfer Playbook (Wyner, Deane, Jenkins, Fink). Tailored advising + prior-learning credit review as evidence-based practices.
  • Monaghan & Attewell (2015). Documented ~50% credit loss during institutional handoff — the empirical case for the Activity 3 Prior Learning & Experience Audit.
  • Ishitani transfer-shock research. First-term GPA dip is predictable; Activities 1–3 in the first six weeks absorb the shock.

Full citations and the 14-row framework attribution table: Program → Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway → Frameworks Cited.

Pilot Status & Anticipated Targets (Fall 2026 Launch)

  • 100% of incoming transfers assigned a Transfer Elevate Success Coach within 2 weeks of enrollment.
  • 85%+ of Transfer Cohort students complete the 9 base activities within their bridge year.
  • Signed Transition Placement Form on file for every transfer before their second academic year.
  • Pre / post administration of the Laanan-Transfer Students’ Questionnaire (L-TSQ) — the validated instrument used to measure change in transfer student capital across the bridge year.
  • Pilot enrollment target: 50–100 transfers — 25–50% of the ~200 incoming per cycle.
  • Transfer graduation-to-employment rate to meet or exceed the broader Elevate benchmark and the national community-college-to-bachelor’s completion rate (Velasco / Fink / Jenkins 2024).

See the Full Bridge Curriculum

For the complete 9-activity sequence, the summer-2026 build-out timeline, the cross-functional partner roster (Transfer Office, Admissions, Markie Lin Rhodes), the framework citation table, and the full APA-7 reference list, see Program → Transfer Cohort Bridge Pathway →.

What has actually happened in the first 14 months of Elevate · and how the architecture is designed to compound over the next four years.

Illustrative Pathway Scenarios — Intended Design Patterns

Intended · Architecture Scenarios

The pathway scenarios below are not actual student journeys. No real student has yet completed these specific badge combinations.

They are illustrative design patterns — showing how the Elevate badge architecture, partnership pipelines, and auto-earn triggers are intended to compound across a student’s four years. Use them to read the program’s logic: how a Stuart Finance student could ladder from SIE exam → Future CFO badge → first-rotation analyst role; how an Armour student could ladder from AWS Academy → IPRO → co-op. These scenarios reflect the design, not the delivered outcome. Real outcomes will be tracked in the longitudinal study (planned 2030 publication).

FERPA notice. The lettered “students” (A., R., J., P., etc.) in the scenarios below are composite design personas — not real students with paraphrased names. They illustrate how the architecture is meant to work for a representative student in each school. No actual student records are referenced. Identifying records of real Elevate participants live exclusively on Campus Groups (312) and are accessed by Career Services staff under FERPA-compliant role-gating.

A. — Stuart School of Business Intended scenario

Finance / SIE Exam → Future CFO Auto-Earn → First-Rotation Analyst

CohortStuart Sophomore (Finance concentration)
Elevate ActivitiesSecurities Industry Essentials (SIE) Exam · Stuart Investments Membership · CMC Career Coaching · Career Fair Prep Squad · Financial Literacy Workshop Series
Badges EarnedFuture CFO (auto-earn at SIE 70%+) · Financial Literacy L3 · Pro Comms L2 · Career Fair Strategist
Framework AnchorsNACE Career & Self-Development + Technology · WEF Analytical Thinking · Coursera 2026 Data Analytics
OutcomeFirst-rotation analyst role secured through Stuart CMC + Elevate Career Fair Prep Squad pipeline.

What it shows. The C-Suite auto-earn architecture pays off when a single industry certification (SIE) collapses three forms of evidence: technical mastery, employer-recognized credential, and a verifiable Elevate badge. A. used Stuart CMC + Elevate as a single pipeline rather than two competing services — exactly the integration model the October 2025 Curt Allee partnership protocol was designed for.

R. — Armour College of Engineering Intended scenario

AWS Academy + IPRO Innovation Day → Engineering Co-op

CohortArmour Junior (Computer / Electrical Engineering)
Elevate ActivitiesAWS Cloud Foundations → Cloud Architect · IPRO Innovation Day · Resume Development (VMock) · Industry Research Deep Dive · LinkedIn Optimization
Badges EarnedAWS Cloud Architect · Cloud Foundations Milestone · Innovation Pioneer · Digital Literacy L4 · Industry Navigator
Framework AnchorsNACE Technology + Critical Thinking · WEF Cloud Computing + AI & Big Data · Coursera 2026 Cybersecurity + Cloud
OutcomeEngineering co-op placement in a Chicago-area cloud-infrastructure team; portfolio includes IPRO project and AWS certification verifiable URL.

What it shows. The AWS-into-IPRO pipeline turns a free certification pathway into employer-visible evidence. R.’s LinkedIn profile carries a Credly badge for the AWS cert and a public IPRO project artifact — two distinct signals that arrive at recruiters before the interview. The IPRO Innovation Day employer-sponsored format is the Elevate counterpart to a Braven Capstone Challenge, demonstrating the “Elevate Capstone Track” thesis in practice.

J. — Lewis College of Science and Letters Intended scenario

Humanities Major → Career-Pivot Workshop Sequence → UX Research Internship

CohortLewis Junior (Humanities + Psychological Science)
Elevate ActivitiesWorkshop 13 Career Pivoting & Transferable Skills · Workshop 5 Informational Interviews · Workshop 6 Networking · Industry Research Deep Dive · Portfolio Development
Badges EarnedCareer Pivot Navigator · Network Navigator · Networking Pro · Career Critical Thinker L2 · Portfolio Builder
Framework AnchorsNACE Career & Self-Development + Communication · CASEL Self-Awareness · WEF Curiosity + Resilience
OutcomeUX research internship landed. Pivot narrative reframes humanities methods (interviewing, qualitative coding, narrative analysis) as transferable UX-research skills.

What it shows. The Workshop 13 + Workshop 5 + Workshop 6 sequence is exactly the “73% of grads work in a different field than their major” problem solved in practice. J.’s case validates the bet that career-readiness scaffolding is most needed in the colleges with the longest distance between major and labor market — Lewis being a primary example.

P. — Transfer Cohort Bridge (Pilot) Intended scenario

Community-College Transfer → Prior-Learning Audit → Y3 Accountability Placement

CohortTransfer Bridge (community-college transfer from City Colleges of Chicago)
Elevate ActivitiesTransfer Orientation + T&C · Transfer Elevate Success Coach First Check-In · Prior Learning & Experience Audit · Handshake + Resume · LinkedIn Optimization · Activity 9 Transition Meeting
Audit Result4 of 6 Y1 + Y2 requirements credited from CC coursework, prior work history, and a documented internship.
Framework AnchorsLaanan TSC · Schlossberg Transition Theory (Moving-In → Moving-Out) · Transfer Receptive Culture · Monaghan & Attewell credit-portability case
OutcomePlaced into Y3 Accountability after the bridge year; on track for graduation in 4 semesters with the same employment guarantee as four-year starters.

What it shows. The Activity 3 Prior Learning Audit is the operational lever that prevents the “~50% credit loss during institutional handoff” national pattern (Monaghan & Attewell, 2015). P.’s placement into Y3 with the same guarantee — not a parallel track, not a lower bar — is the policy promise of the Transfer Receptive Culture framework made operational.

Additional Pathway Stories

M. — Stuart (Marketing Track)

AMA Membership + Marketing Analytics Project + Professional Communications L3 → Future CMO badge cluster. Landed agency-side internship through Stuart Career Fair + LinkedIn outreach pipeline.

S. — Computing / ITM

AWS Security Specialty + Tech+ Integration Specialist project + ITM Cybersecurity coursework → Future CTO pathway components. Cybersecurity internship at a Chicago-area MSP secured via Industry Research + Network Navigator badges.

N. — Pre-Health (Lewis)

Service Learning Abroad + Pre-Health badge track + Community Impact Leader badge → medical-school application strengthened by Global Citizen and Community Champion evidence. Acceptance to two regional medical schools.

D. — Armour (Civil Eng.)

Sargent & Lundy employer pipeline (anchor partner) + ELM Leadership L2 + Industry Certification (PMI CAPM) → Future COO auto-earn. Co-op offer converted to full-time post-graduation.

K. — Architecture

Faculty-Led Short-Term Study Away + Portfolio Development Workshop + Globe Trotter International → Global Citizen + Cultural Intelligence Leader. Studio practice abroad cited in NAAB portfolio review.

T. — Commuter (99% Y2)

Built an entirely async badge portfolio: Industry Podcast Series + Virtual Informational Interviews + AI Tools for Productivity + Digital Portfolio → Digital Literacy L3 + Industry Navigator. Validates the commuter-first elective design.

L. — First-Gen + International

Workshop 9 Personal Branding for the AI Era + Workshop 11 Interview Prep + AMA Chapter → OPT-eligible role secured. Personal-brand narrative reframed cross-cultural background as a professional asset.

B. — Braven Fellow (Sophomore)

BRVN 200 + Leadership Academy + Career Pivoting Workshop → Professional Communications L3 + Collaboration Champion + Career Critical Thinker. Confirms the stacked-impact thesis: Braven inside Elevate produces both credentials.

Cross-Story Patterns — What These Journeys Have in Common

1. The auto-earn flywheel works.

When an industry certification (SIE, CAPM, AWS) doubles as a C-Suite badge auto-trigger, students stop seeing Elevate as “extra” work and start seeing it as a credential multiplier. A., D., and S. all demonstrate this.

2. Workshops + Activities > Workshops alone.

J. and L. show that the 13 Workshops + a structured activity (Industry Research Deep Dive, AMA Chapter) produce a portfolio-grade artifact — not just a learning outcome. The workshops scaffold; the activities deliver evidence.

3. Equity built-in pays off.

P. (transfer), T. (commuter), L. (first-gen + international), N. (pre-health service-learning) all required the deliberate equity-by-design choices in the Elevate catalog — async electives, the Transfer Bridge, the Wild Card / Flight Plan custom pathway. Without them, those outcomes don’t happen.

4. Partnerships pull students through.

AWS Academy (R., S., D.), Stuart CMC (A., M.), Braven (B.), Study Away (K., N.) — every Tier 3 / Tier 4 partnership shows up across the story set as the gravity well that pulls students into deeper engagement. Partnerships are not a side bet; they are the engine.

5. The coach relationship is the constant.

In every story above, a Elevate Success Coach intake was the first checkpoint — the moment where a Co-Curricular Plan turned a list of activities into a coherent year. The coach is not an extra service; the coach is the operating system.

6. Verifiable beats narrative.

Every successful outcome carried at least one externally verifiable artifact: an AWS Credly badge, an SIE exam score, a Stuart CMC sign-off, a published IPRO project, a LinkedIn-shareable portfolio URL. The Open Badges 3.0 / Verifiable Credentials roadmap is the institutional commitment to making that the default for every Elevate student by 2027.

Longitudinal Outcome Tracking — What Comes Next

Elevate launched in March 2025; multi-year graduate outcome data will not be available until the first full cohort graduates. The plan is to track every Elevate alumnus from Day 1 of graduation through five years out — employment status, salary band, industry sector, role-to-major fit, graduate school enrollment, and self-reported career satisfaction. By 2030, Elevate’s longitudinal dataset will be Illinois-Tech-specific, framework-anchored, and durable.

Comparators. Outcome benchmarks are set against (1) NACE national first-destination survey data, (2) the Velasco / Fink / Jenkins community-college-to-bachelor’s completion benchmark, (3) the Braven 89% employed-or-grad-school-at-6-months benchmark, and (4) the Coursera 2026 employer-demand priority skills. The five-year Elevate alumni study (planned 2030 publication) is the durable artifact behind the employment guarantee.

Competitive Landscape — Global Comparative Analysis

A scholarly comparison of the Illinois Tech Elevate Initiative against the leading career-readiness, experiential-learning, and co-operative education programs at top-tier universities and research centers worldwide · including the institutional programs referenced in Elevate’s grounding frameworks.

Methodology

This comparative analysis benchmarks the Elevate initiative against five categories of established career-readiness and experiential-learning programs: (1) North American cooperative-education programs with century-plus operating histories, (2) leading research universities whose career and experiential-learning offices anchor the field, (3) international experiential-learning programs at top global institutions, (4) equity-focused career-preparation programs (with which Elevate stacks rather than competes), and (5) longitudinal career-outcomes research initiatives.

Every institutional program named in Elevate’s eight grounding frameworks plus the institutional-model bibliography is included by design, so that this page doubles as a benchmark map between Elevate’s evidence base and the operating programs that produced that evidence.

1. North American Cooperative-Education & Experiential-Learning Programs

Institution & ProgramFounding / ScopeElevate Comparison
University of Cincinnati Cooperative Education1906 — originator of cooperative education (Herman Schneider). Mandatory co-op rotations across engineering, business, design.Elevate scales the Cincinnati co-op rationale across all five Illinois Tech schools with badge verifiability + the Elevate employment guarantee.
Northeastern University Co-op & Experiential Learning1909 — 100+ years of alternating semesters with full-time paid work; 4,000+ employer partners; widely considered the gold standard for university-scale co-op.Elevate offers a structured four-year framework with verified badges rather than a single co-op model; co-ops, internships, and micro-internships all live inside the Elevate catalog as Y3 / Y4 pathways.
Drexel University Co-op1919 — five-year program with three six-month co-ops; alternating semester model adapted from Cincinnati.Drexel’s six-month rotation length informs Elevate’s Y3 / Y4 internship and co-op verification rubric.
Rochester Institute of Technology (RIT) Cooperative Education1912 — one of the largest career-services and co-op operations in the U.S. by student volume; mandatory across most undergraduate programs.Elevate’s C-Suite stacked-badge model is the credentialing layer absent from RIT’s pure-co-op approach.
Antioch College Cooperative Education1921 — co-founder of the U.S. co-op model with Cincinnati and Northeastern; six off-campus co-op terms required.Antioch’s required-rotation model is the historical antecedent for the Elevate four-year experience-requirement architecture.
Georgia Institute of Technology Career CenterCited in Elevate’s institutional-model framework. Co-op + internship infrastructure across engineering, computing, business.Georgia Tech’s technical-employer pipeline is a direct model for Elevate’s anchor-employer integration (Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Microsoft, Accenture).
University of Waterloo Co-op (Canada)1957 — world’s largest post-secondary co-op program, >25,000 students annually across 7,300 employer partners; six terms of paid co-op in most programs.Waterloo sets the global ceiling on co-op scale. Elevate’s differentiation is breadth (80+ activity types, not just co-op) and four-year-arc continuity.
Worcester Polytechnic Institute (WPI) Project-Based LearningThe WPI Plan (1970) — three required interactive qualifying projects + major qualifying project; project-based curriculum widely benchmarked.IPRO Innovation Day at Illinois Tech is the structural analogue; Elevate codifies and credentials it across all schools.
Olin College of EngineeringFounded 1997 — entirely project-based engineering curriculum; partners with Stanford d.school and MIT Media Lab.Olin is the most-cited contemporary model for integrating project-based learning institution-wide; informs Elevate Capstone Track design.

2. U.S. Research University Career & Experiential-Learning Offices

Institution & ProgramFounding / ScopeElevate Comparison
Harvard Graduate School of Education — Pathways to ProsperityCited in Elevate frameworks. National initiative advocating structured pathways from secondary education through career; emphasizes guided pathway design.Elevate operationalizes the Pathways to Prosperity rationale at the Illinois Tech scale — Pre-College through senior year + Transfer Bridge.
Harvard Business School — Managing the Future of WorkCited in Elevate frameworks. Research initiative on skills-based hiring, experiential credentialing, and the future of work.Elevate’s 73-badge skills-based architecture is the campus-scale implementation of the HBS Managing the Future of Work thesis.
MIT Experiential Learning Office (ELO)Cited in Elevate frameworks. Multi-pathway portfolio model coordinating UROP, MISTI international internships, Sloan Action Learning, and MITdesignX entrepreneurship.The MIT ELO multi-pathway model directly informs Elevate’s “menu of activities anchored to a single framework” design.
MIT Teaching + Learning Lab — Digital CredentialingCited in Elevate frameworks. Pioneer in Open Badges 3.0, Digital Credentials Consortium, and Verifiable Credentials research.Elevate’s Open Badges 3.0 + Verifiable Credentials roadmap aligns directly with MIT T+L Lab’s standards work.
Stanford Career Education + Stanford d.schoolCareer Education + the Hasso Plattner Institute of Design (d.school) pioneered design-thinking-anchored career development and life-design curricula.Workshop 2 (CAR Method) and Workshop 13 (Career Pivoting) in the Elevate catalog adopt Stanford’s design-thinking-applied-to-careers methodology.
Carnegie Mellon University — Career & Professional Development CenterHighly competitive employer pipeline; strong tech-recruiter penetration; cross-college integration model.CMU’s cross-college integration is the structural model Elevate uses to coordinate Stuart, Armour, Computing, Lewis, and Architecture under one framework.
Purdue University — Purdue-Gallup IndexCited in Elevate frameworks. Longitudinal study (2014–present) linking experiential learning, mentorship, and applied projects to long-term well-being and engagement.Elevate is the operational implementation that produces the data Purdue-Gallup measures; the 2030 Elevate longitudinal study is designed against Purdue-Gallup methodology.

3. International Experiential-Learning & Career-Readiness Programs

Institution & ProgramCountry / ScopeElevate Comparison
Oxford University Careers ServiceUK — cited in Elevate frameworks. Skills-based career-development framework; bespoke college tutorial system integrated with central careers service.Oxford’s skills-based framework directly informs Elevate’s 5-track foundational badge model and cross-school skills mapping.
University of Cambridge Careers ServiceUK — subject-specific careers advisers + comprehensive employer engagement program; rigorous outcomes tracking via Graduate Outcomes survey (HESA).Cambridge’s longitudinal outcomes tracking via the HESA Graduate Outcomes survey is a methodological benchmark for the Elevate 2030 longitudinal study.
Imperial College London Careers ServiceUK — STEM-focused, employer-engaged careers service with strong industry advisory boards; deep professional-society integration (IEEE, IET, etc.).Imperial’s professional-society integration is the operational template for Elevate’s AMA, PM Excellence Club, and professional-association activity recognition.
ETH Zurich Career CenterSwitzerland — STEM-anchored career service for one of the top European technical universities; strong industry-PhD pipeline.ETH’s industry-academic seamlessness is a model for Elevate’s quantum-computing (Chicago Quantum Exchange) and AWS Academy partnerships.
National University of Singapore (NUS) Centre for Future-ready GraduatesSingapore — comprehensive career-readiness curriculum with embedded competencies-based development model; strong APAC industry network.NUS’s competencies-based career curriculum is the closest non-U.S. parallel to Elevate’s NACE 8 + WEF 2030 anchor model.
University of Tokyo Career SupportJapan — integrated career support across academic departments; strong domestic industry placement plus international corporate partnerships.Tokyo’s domestic-industry-first model informs Elevate’s Chicago-and-Midwest employer pipeline + the international-student same-country alumni-mentor match.
University of Melbourne Careers & IndustryAustralia — structured Industry Mentoring Program (annual cohort) + cross-institutional employability framework.Melbourne’s annual Industry Mentoring Program cohort structure is the most direct international template for the Elevate alumni-mentor activation roadmap.

4. Equity-Focused Career-Preparation Programs (Stacked with Elevate, Not Competing)

ProgramScopeElevate Comparison
Braven Career & Leadership AcceleratorNational nonprofit — one-semester accelerator at 7+ partner schools; 15,500+ Fellows; 57% strong-first-job rate (12 pts above national avg).Braven sits inside Elevate as a Year 2 Sophomore Elevate Experience option (BRVN 200, Fall 2026). Elevate is the four-year framework that makes Braven’s contribution durable.
Year UpNational workforce-development program for young adults 18–29; 12-month program combining technical training + internship.Year Up serves a non-overlapping population (pre-college / community-college learners); Elevate’s Pre-College pathway absorbs the equivalent rationale for matriculated Illinois Tech students.
College Possible / 10,000 Degrees / Bottom LineNational college-success and persistence support for first-gen and low-income students; coaching-anchored model.Coaching-anchored design parallels Elevate’s Elevate Success Coach model; Elevate adds the badge architecture and the four-year framework these programs lack.
America Needs You / iMentorMentorship-anchored programs for first-gen and underrepresented students; pre-college through early career.Elevate’s Alumni Mentorship Activation roadmap (Y3 + Y4) adopts the iMentor / America Needs You matching model at the institutional scale.
ColorStack / Out for UndergradIdentity-aligned career-readiness networks (CS / engineering for Black, Latine, Indigenous students; LGBTQ+ leadership).Affinity-pod alumni mentorship (idea 7 in the activation roadmap) is the institutional answer to identity-aligned career-readiness networks.

5. Longitudinal Career-Outcomes Research & Indices

InitiativeScopeElevate Comparison
Purdue-Gallup Index2014–present. Longitudinal index linking experiential learning, mentorship, and applied projects to graduate well-being and workplace engagement.Elevate is the operational program that generates the kind of data Purdue-Gallup measures; designed as Illinois Tech’s campus-scale Purdue-Gallup parallel.
NACE First-Destination SurveyAnnual national first-destination outcomes for U.S. bachelor’s graduates — the most widely cited benchmark for graduate employment / grad-school status at 6 months.Elevate’s 2030 longitudinal study reports against NACE First-Destination methodology so peer-institution comparison remains apples-to-apples.
CCRC / Aspen Tracking TransferVelasco, Fink & Jenkins (2024). Community-college-to-bachelor’s completion benchmark; cited in Elevate Transfer Bridge frameworks.Sets the external completion benchmark against which the Transfer Cohort Bridge 85%+ completion goal is measured.
U.K. HESA Graduate Outcomes SurveyU.K. national survey of graduate destinations 15 months post-graduation; methodological benchmark for outcome attribution.Methodological reference point for Elevate’s 15-month and 5-year post-graduation tracking design.
Burning Glass Technologies / Lightcast Skills DataReal-time labor-market skills data from millions of job postings; cited in Elevate frameworks (Hybrid Job Economy report, 2019).Real-time labor-market skill demand cross-walked against Elevate badge architecture quarterly to keep the 73-badge catalog current with hiring signals.

Where Elevate Holds an Institutional Position No Peer Holds

DimensionStrongest Peer Holding the PositionElevate Position
Co-op scaleWaterloo (Canada) · Northeastern (US)Illinois Tech doesn’t compete on co-op-as-centerpiece scale. Co-ops here are a structured Career Services cooperative-education program — paid, credit-bearing, full-time — facilitated through the Internship & Co-Op channel. Elevate’s role is credentialing: the Internship / Co-Op Champion milestone recognizes co-op completion, and co-ops sit alongside internships, capstones, IPRO, and research as structured experiential pathways within Elevate’s four-year framework.
Project-based learningWPI · Olin CollegeIPRO Innovation Day is Illinois Tech’s WPI-equivalent; Elevate codifies and credentials it.
Skills-based career frameworkOxford · NUS (Singapore)Elevate’s 73-badge framework + 8 grounding frameworks is the U.S. institutional analogue at full operational scale.
Digital credentialing researchMIT T+L Lab · MIT Digital Credentials ConsortiumElevate is the campus-scale implementation site for the MIT-led Open Badges 3.0 / Verifiable Credentials standards.
Equity-focused acceleratorBraven · Year Up · College PossibleElevate stacks rather than competes; Braven sits inside Elevate as Y2; Year Up + College Possible feed Elevate’s Pre-College pathway.
Longitudinal outcomes dataPurdue-Gallup · NACE First-Destination · HESA Graduate OutcomesElevate 2030 longitudinal study designed against Purdue-Gallup, NACE, and HESA methodologies for cross-institutional comparison.
Employment guaranteeNo major-university peer offers an explicit employment guarantee.Elevate’s employment guarantee (6-month placement or funded continuing-education) is institutionally unique.
Cross-school institutional coordinationCarnegie Mellon · MIT ELOElevate is the framework that absorbs Stuart CMC, Braven, Kaplan Institute, AWS Academy, and emerging school-based courses (e.g., Stuart’s developing offering) into one progression.

Strategic Frame

Other institutions offer career services. Illinois Tech guarantees career preparation. The Elevate Initiative occupies a deliberately distinctive position in the global career-readiness landscape: it is not the largest co-op program (Waterloo, Northeastern), it is not the most-iterated career-accelerator course (Braven), and it is not the most-cited longitudinal index (Purdue-Gallup). Instead, Elevate is the institutional framework that absorbs and coordinates the best ideas from each of those peers and renders them as a verifiable four-year credential portfolio backed by an employment guarantee.

The combination is the differentiator: badge verifiability (MIT-aligned), framework rigor (8 grounding frameworks, 25+ APA-cited sources), partnership depth (Stuart 18 activities, AWS 17 certs, Braven inside the catalog, Kaplan, Sargent & Lundy, Chicago Quantum Exchange), equity-by-design (Transfer Bridge, Commuter pathway, async electives), and the institutional Elevate Guarantee — none of which is replicated, in combination, by any peer institution domestically or internationally.

Active across five academic schools · 17 certification pathways · Free vouchers

Scaling Goal

Five active foundational + advanced certification tracks: Cloud Foundations, Cloud Architect, Cloud Developer, Cloud Operations, Machine Learning Foundations. Plus Generative AI, AI Foundations, Cloud Security, Data Engineering, NLP, Security Engineering, Operations Technician, Data Center Tech.

Operating model: 4-week cohorts in summer 2026; 8-week cohorts in fall and spring aligned with the academic calendar. Wednesday office hours at the Smart Lab (Kaplan Institute). Commitment clause at sign-up.

Coordination team: Jeremy Hajek, James Papademas, Hariom Sahu (Illinois Tech ITM faculty); Nureni Adeyemo (AWS Technical Program Manager). AWS office visit conducted April 29, 2026.

Entrepreneurship integration · Chicago innovation ecosystem partner

Primary partner contact: Edo — coordinating credit-bearing pathway exploration and Faculty Summer Camp integration. Smart Lab at Kaplan also hosts AWS Academy Wednesday office hours.

Chicago Innovation Ecosystem — Partner Network

Elevate’s Chicago Innovation Ecosystem Explorer activity (8–15 hours) requires students to visit at least 3 of the following partner organizations and submit a 500-word reflection.

1871

Chicago’s flagship tech innovation hub (Merchandise Mart). Startup incubator + co-working space + member events.

mHUB

Hardtech / hardware innovation center on the Near West Side. Prototyping labs and hardware-startup mentorship.

Founders Institute (Chicago)

Pre-seed startup accelerator. Cohort-based programming for first-time founders.

TechRise

P33 / Pritzker Traubert Foundation initiative supporting Black and Latinx founders.

WBDC (Women’s Business Development Center)

Resources, capital access, and programming for women-led ventures.

Matter

Healthcare-innovation hub at the Merchandise Mart.

UTP (University Technology Park)

Illinois Tech’s on-campus innovation district housing startups, incubators, and research labs.

Future Founders

Youth-entrepreneurship nonprofit; programming and mentorship for student founders.

Pre-Health Partnership

Clinical exposure, research, and graduate-school readiness for pre-health students

Proposed
Badge Pathway Status
AY 2026–27
Launch Target
3
Pillar Activity Themes

Three activity pillars: clinical exposure (shadowing, volunteer hospital service), research (lab placements, undergraduate research credit), and graduate-school readiness (MCAT prep, application portfolios, personal statements).

Status: Dashboard page created; activity catalog scoping in progress with Pre-Health advising. Badge framework will mirror the Stuart C-Suite model — stacked culminating badges (Future MD/Future DO/Future PA pathways) earned through curated activity sequences.

Three Activity Pillars

1. Clinical Exposure

  • Physician / PA / DO shadowing (documented hours log)
  • Volunteer hospital service (Northwestern, Rush, UChicago Medicine, Stroger)
  • Free clinic / community health volunteering
  • EMT / CNA certification + active practice
  • Hospice or palliative-care volunteer service

2. Research

  • Faculty-mentored undergraduate research (Armour, BME, Chemistry)
  • NIH / NSF summer research programs
  • Independent literature review with PI sign-off
  • Poster or conference presentation
  • Co-authored publication credit

3. Graduate-School Readiness

  • MCAT / DAT / OAT / PCAT prep program completion
  • Application portfolio development (personal statement, secondaries)
  • Mock medical school / professional-program interview
  • Pre-health committee letter preparation
  • Casper / AAMC PREview practice + reflection

Proposed Pre-Health C-Suite-Style Badges

PathwayRequirements (Proposed)Auto-Earn
Future MDAll 3 pillars; 100+ shadowing hours; MCAT completion; faculty research letterMCAT score within target range
Future DOAll 3 pillars; DO physician shadowing; OMT exposure; AACOMAS readinessDAT / OAT competency
Future PAAll 3 pillars; 1000+ patient-contact hours (CNA, EMT, scribe); CASPA readyDocumented PCE hours threshold
Future PharmacistPillars 1 + 3; pharmacy tech experience; PCAT prepPCAT competency
Future DDS / DMDAll 3 pillars; DAT prep; dental shadowingDAT competency

Roadmap (AY 2026–2027)

  • Summer 2026: Confirm Pre-Health advising lead; finalize 3-pillar activity inventory; design Future MD/DO/PA badge requirements.
  • Fall 2026: Pilot with first Pre-Health cohort; partner with Chicago hospital systems for shadowing placements.
  • Spring 2027: Full launch; integrate with NIH / NSF research-program calendar; align with health-professions advising committee letter cycle.

Architecture Partnership

College of Architecture outreach has stalled across the Spring 2026 semester — recommending senior-leadership re-engagement via BJ Engelhardt and Mallik Sundharam.

Six rounds of outreach from Career Services since the Feb 4 2026 introduction have not converted to a meeting commitment. CoA workload through midterms and the end of spring is the most likely explanation, but the partnership opportunity is too high-value to drift into Fall. We propose BJ Engelhardt (Executive Director, Career Services & Experiential Learning) and Mallik Sundharam open a peer-level conversation with Dean Reed Kroloff to designate a dedicated CoA faculty / staff lead and unblock the activity-design phase. Career Services will provide a one-page brief, framework-aligned activity slate, and a 60-second precedent video so the senior conversation can be brief and decision-oriented.

Professional guardrails for the escalation

  • Frame the request as a capacity unlock, not as a complaint about CoA responsiveness. Spring is documented as a high-load semester for the College of Architecture.
  • Lead with the opportunity (5 framework-aligned activities ready to scope; precedent set with Stuart, Armour, Kaplan, Pre-Health) before raising the unblocking ask.
  • Make it easy to say yes: Career Services will lead all design / verification / publishing work; CoA contribution is a designated lead + 30-min scoping session.
  • Respect the existing handoff: Reed designated Kathy + Mike on Mar 2. The senior conversation re-affirms that handoff and asks Reed to confirm bandwidth or designate an alternate.
  • Document every step of the escalation in the timeline table above so the partnership archive remains executive-readable through future cycles.

Current partnership status: STALLED — senior re-engagement requested

Braven Professional Development Partnership

BRVN 200: Braven Career Success · 3-credit hybrid course launching Fall 2026 · A Year 2 Elevate experience option that builds career-ready skills, confidence, and professional networks.

15,500+
Braven Fellows Nationwide
57%
Secure Strong First Jobs (12pts above national avg)
+22pts
More Likely to Have Internships
92%
On-Time Graduation Rate

Strategic Positioning

“Braven is one of the most impactful experiences inside Elevate. Elevate is the four-year framework that makes Braven count.”

Per Cynthia Stewart’s confirmation, BRVN 200 is a Year 2 Sophomore Elevate Experience option — visible within the Elevate progression rather than as a parallel track. Braven completion counts toward Elevate badging.

Build Career-Ready Skills with BRVN 200

Illinois Tech is launching BRVN 200: Braven Career Success this Fall 2026 in partnership with Braven — a national organization that supports students in landing strong jobs after college. Through a mix of online learning, hands-on assignments, and weekly small-group mentorship, students — known as fellows — graduate from the course more confident, prepared, and connected.

This course is designed especially for second-year, third-year, and transfer students. During the course, fellows will: build a professional portfolio (resume, LinkedIn profile, cover letter); learn and grow in small, interactive cohorts led by a Leadership Coach; collaborate on a real-world Capstone Challenge with an employer partner; practice interview skills during mock interviews with professionals; and earn 3 credits that count toward graduation.

Eligibility & Schedule

WhoSophomores, Juniors & Transfer Students
MajorNo declared major required
Credits3 credits (counts toward GPA)
FormatHybrid — primarily virtual with 3 in-person sessions
Section 1Wednesdays 5:00–7:00 PM
Section 2Thursdays 3:15–5:15 PM
OfferedFall semesters only

How the Career Accelerator with Braven Works

Braven Career Success is divided into three phases. Throughout the course, fellows are grouped into small cohorts with other Illinois Tech students from various majors, allowing them to connect across disciplines and develop teamwork skills that mirror the real world.

PHASE 1

Design Your Career

Bond with your cohort of fellows by sharing core value stories. Explore leadership, values, and career interests to create a career project plan. Complete the “Find My Ikigai” exercise to discover the intersection of what you enjoy, what you’re good at, and what careers align with both.

PHASE 2

Launch Your Career

Build or refine your resume, develop interview skills through mock interviews and informational interviews with professionals, and learn to communicate your story clearly and concisely. Receive extensive feedback on cover letters and practice your skills at networking events.

PHASE 3

Capstone Challenge

Partner with a local employer to tackle a real-world business challenge over five weeks. Use design thinking to analyze the problem, develop actionable solutions, and present your recommendations to a panel of professionals — gaining direct exposure to industry expectations.

In-Class Activities — Group Work

  • Learning Labs. 2-hour weekly discussions (synchronous)
  • Capstone. One group Capstone Challenge project

Out-of-Class Activities — Independent Work

  • Online Modules. 90–120 minutes weekly
  • Portfolio. Weekly submissions
  • Coaching. One 1:1 conversation with your Leadership Coach

Is This Course Right for You?

In all cases, BRVN 200 satisfies a free elective in any student’s degree. You will have to dedicate time to prepare for internships, grad school, or your first job — now or later. Why wait? Earn credit while building your skills, growing your network, and getting career-ready.

Starting Fall 2026, BRVN 200 will be:

  • Required for Hope Scholars and Chicago Difference sophomores and juniors
  • Recommended as a curricular option for sophomores, transfer students, or juniors to fulfill:
    • Elevate’s Experiential Opportunities or Optional Experience
    • Elevate’s co-op and internship preparation requirement for second-year, third-year, and transfer students

Elevate badges you can earn through BRVN 200: Professional Communications · Career Critical Thinker · Collaboration Champion · Leadership Development.

Elevate vs Braven — 14-Dimension Side-by-Side

The structural and strategic differences between Elevate and Braven across the dimensions Career Services tracks. The strongest positioning for both is stacked, not substituted — Braven is one of the most impactful experiences inside Elevate, and Elevate is the four-year experience that makes Braven’s contribution durable.

DimensionElevateBravenAdvantage
DurationFour years (Pre-College through senior year) — structured developmental progression with milestone badges and tier escalationOne semester (3 credits) — intensive but time-limitedElevate
Cost to StudentFree. Employment guarantee eligibility upon completion.Tuition-bearing (3 credits). Cost implications for commuters and first-gen students.Elevate
Academic CreditIn development — credit-bearing pathway via Kaplan Institute and Stuart School conversations underwayYes — transcript-recognized graded courseBraven
Activity Choice80+ activities across foundational, milestone, specialized, and Transfer pilot tracks. Student self-selects based on career trajectory.Single fixed curriculum with no menu options within the courseElevate
Cohort CoachingElevate Success Coaches across all 4 years. Working-professional coach layer planned using Illinois Tech employer relationships.One-semester Leadership Coach (working professional) assigned per cohort of 5–8 FellowsBuilding
Capstone FormatMultiple paths: AWS Academy projects, Kaplan venture work, IPRO Innovation Day, micro-internships, co-ops, job shadows. Capstone Track in development.Five-week employer-sponsored design-thinking challenge with presentation to sponsor companyBoth
MentorshipFour-year tiered model (in development): FYE Y1, Elevate Success Coach all years, alumni matching Y2–Y3, industry mentor Y412–15 week 1:1 professional mentor post-course, timed to senior-year job searchBuilding
Alumni Network80,000+ living Illinois Tech alumni across all industries. Activation infrastructure in development.10,000+ Braven Fellows across partner schoolsElevate
Curriculum Foundation8 nationally recognized frameworks (NACE 92%, P21 95%, WEF 88%, AAC&U 82%, O*NET 85%, CASEL 78%, DOE 75%, Kolb 100%) + 25+ APA-cited institutional modelsOne iterated course curriculum refined over 10+ years. Modules, labs, and Capstone format are mature.Both
Outcome DataBuilding — launched March 2025. Longitudinal study planned through 2030. Real-time dashboard tracks engagement now.10+ years of published, third-party-evaluated data. 89% employed/grad school at 6 months. 75% out-earning parents.Braven
Institutional FitBuilt for Illinois Tech. Integrates with Career Services, AWS Academy, Kaplan Institute, SQMS at Fermilab, Stuart CMC, and Illinois Tech employer pipelines. Can pivot with Illinois Tech priorities.National nonprofit running the same model at 7+ schools. Cannot pivot for any one campus.Elevate
Equity & AccessibilityBuilt for commuters, working students, and transfers. Async/flex options. Transfer pilot architecture. Cohort-flexibility model.Hybrid format with fixed lab times. Can conflict with work and class schedules.Elevate
Badge / Credential System73-badge framework: 25 foundational, 20 milestone, 28 specialized. Visible on LinkedIn. Real-time dashboard tracking.Course completion + Fellow status — a single credential, not a portfolioElevate
Cross-Institutional CoordinationActs as the institutional framework — absorbs school-specific courses (Stuart, Braven, future offerings) into one progression. Prevents fragmentation.Standalone course. Does not coordinate with other career-readiness efforts across schools.Elevate

Where Elevate Outshines Braven (7 Dimensions)

1. Four-Year Developmental Arc

Elevate runs across the entire student journey — Pre-College through senior year and the Transfer pilot — with milestone badges, tier escalation, and cumulative skill-building. Braven is one semester, three credits, and the formal course is done.

2. Breadth of Activities & Choice Architecture

Elevate offers 80+ activities; students self-select based on their career trajectory — commuter-friendly options, Chicago Adventures, AWS Academy, Esports activities, Kaplan Startup Studio, IPRO Innovation Day. Braven is a single fixed curriculum.

3. Personalization & Equity Built In

Elevate accommodates commuters, working students, transfer students, and students with caregiving responsibilities through async options, cohort flexibility, and the Transfer pilot architecture. Braven’s hybrid format has fixed lab times that can conflict with work and class.

4. Institutional Fit & Strategic Ownership

Elevate integrates with Career Services, AWS Academy, the Kaplan Institute, the quantum partnerships, and Illinois Tech’s Chicago / global employer pipelines. It can pivot when Illinois Tech’s strategic priorities shift. Braven cannot pivot for any one campus.

5. Cross-Cohort Flexibility & Continuity

Elevate Success Coaches are trained across the full catalog — continuity lives in the system and the team. Braven cohorts depend on a single Leadership Coach for the semester; if that coach leaves or underperforms, the cohort experience suffers.

6. Cost to the Student

Elevate is free, with employment-guarantee eligibility upon completion. Braven is a 3-credit course with tuition implications — a real distinction for commuters and first-generation students paying out of pocket.

7. Integrated Dashboard & Badge Architecture

Students see their progress, badges earned, and tier advancement in real time on the Elevate dashboard. The 73-badge framework (25 foundational + 20 milestone + 28 specialized) gives them a visible micro-credential portfolio for LinkedIn. Braven offers course completion + Fellow status — a single credential.

Where Braven Has Strengths — and How Elevate Is Approaching Them

1. Credit-Bearing, Transcript-Recognized

Braven’s strength: Course completion appears on the academic transcript as a graded credit.

Elevate’s trajectory: Credit-bearing integration is an active milestone on the roadmap via Kaplan and Stuart conversations. In the interim, Braven’s credit-bearing structure plugs directly into Elevate as the Year 2 credit-bearing option.

2. Working-Professional Leadership Coach

Braven’s strength: Each cohort of 5–8 Fellows is led by a working professional from a partner company.

Elevate’s trajectory: Future iterations layer working-professional volunteer coaches on top of Elevate Success Coaches — sourced from Illinois Tech’s Chicago employer relationships, alumni base, and partner network (Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Kaplan, Fermilab).

3. Capstone Challenge With an Employer

Braven’s strength: Five-week employer-sponsored design-thinking project culminating in a presentation to the sponsor company.

Elevate’s trajectory: Job shadows, micro-internships, co-ops, AWS Academy projects, Kaplan Startup Studio, and IPRO Innovation Day are existing employer-facing project options — ready to package into an Elevate Capstone Track.

4. National Alumni Network

Braven’s strength: 10,000+ Fellow network across multiple universities.

Elevate’s reality: Illinois Tech has 80,000+ living alumni — roughly 8× Braven’s national fellowship base. The work ahead is alumni-Elevate mentorship matching, alumni-led workshops, and alumni-sponsored Capstone projects.

5. Post-Course Professional Mentor

Braven’s strength: 12–15 week 1:1 professional mentorship timed to senior-year job search.

Elevate’s trajectory: Four years of continuous mentorship — Elevate Success Coach across all four years, FYE first-year mentoring, alumni mentor matching in upper years, and industry mentor connections through partner companies.

6. Curriculum Maturity

Braven’s strength: A decade of iteration on a single course design — modules, lab structure, and Capstone format are refined.

Elevate’s foundation: Synthesis of 25+ APA-cited frameworks, theories, and institutional models across NACE, P21, WEF, AAC&U, O*NET, CASEL, DOE, and Kolb — single-course depth vs. cross-framework breadth.

7. Outcome Data With Credibility (Acknowledged Gap)

Braven’s strength: A decade of published, third-party-evaluated outcome data — 89% employment / grad school at 6 months, 75% out-earning parents, 23-point internship attainment lift.

Elevate’s reality: Launched March 2025; longitudinal outcome data builds toward 2030. The mitigation is the data infrastructure to track Elevate alumni from Day 1 of graduation.

It Doesn’t End When the Course Does

After completing the Braven Career Success course, fellows gain continued access to leadership and career development opportunities, networking events, and a national community of peers and professionals. Fellows receive access to MyBraven — Braven’s all-in-one digital platform where fellows can connect with staff, access resources, and stay engaged with career development tools.

  • MyBraven Platform. All-in-one digital hub for resources and connections
  • Professional Mentors. Optional 12-week 1:1 mentoring program
  • Career Communities. Join up to 2 (STEM, Business, Health, Arts & Media, Public Service, Explorers)
  • Career Coaching. Additional 1:1 coaching sessions
  • Employer Events. Virtual and in-person hiring and networking events
  • Opportunities Newsletter. Internship and job referrals
  • Talent Directory. Exclusive access for Braven graduates
  • Ready-to-Launch. Senior-year bootcamp for hands-on job application support

Illinois Tech

Frequently Asked Questions

QuestionResponse
Who is eligible?Sophomores, juniors, and transfer students. You do not need a declared major to enroll.
When is it offered?Fall semesters only. Two sections: Wednesdays 5:00–7:00 PM or Thursdays 3:15–5:15 PM. If you have scheduling conflicts, consider the other section.
Does it affect my GPA?Yes. BRVN 200 is a 3-credit course that factors into your GPA. Full attendance and participation are expected. The course can only be taken once.
Can this help me decide on a major?Yes — the course includes “Find My Ikigai,” a framework that helps you explore the intersection of what you enjoy, what you’re good at, and what careers align with both. You’ll also create a career roadmap to navigate academic and career pathways at Illinois Tech.
How is this different from other courses?This isn’t a lecture. You’ll be part of a small, tight-knit group of 5–8 students, working together throughout the semester. Each group is led by a Leadership Coach — a working professional who brings real-world perspective into every session. The course is built around collaboration and active participation.
Will Braven place me in a job or internship?Braven is not a placement organization. Instead, they ensure you have the confidence, skills, experiences, and networks to apply and succeed. Students also receive access to an opportunities newsletter and Braven’s exclusive Talent Directory.
What’s expected in virtual sessions?Camera on, active participation, and full engagement — just as you would in person. Being fully present and minimizing distractions will help you maximize your relationship with your Leadership Coach and peers.
Does this count toward Elevate?Yes. BRVN 200 qualifies as an Elevate requirement or optional experience. It also satisfies the required career component for Chicago Difference and Hope Chicago scholars. Ask your Elevate Student Elevate Success Coach for details on how it fits into your specific checklist.

A Broader Trend: Why Elevate’s Framework Role Matters Now

Stuart School of Business is currently developing its own course modeled on the Braven approach, situated within its own academic structure. That development confirms the appetite for credit-bearing, cohort-based career-readiness coursework is institutional, not isolated to one external partnership.

As more colleges, schools, and departments build their own career-readiness courses, Illinois Tech risks duplication, fragmentation, and inconsistent student experience. This is precisely where Elevate’s role as the institutional framework becomes most strategically valuable. Elevate is not a course — it is the four-year career-readiness architecture that absorbs and coordinates discipline-specific offerings, ensures consistency across Illinois Tech, and gives students a single visible badge progression regardless of which college’s course they take.

The strategic question shifts from “do we need Braven or Elevate?” to “which courses, partnerships, and credentials live within Elevate as the framework that holds the four-year experience together?”

Recommended Next Steps

#ActionStatus
1Confirm Braven’s role in the Elevate dashboard — positioned as a Year 2 Sophomore Elevate Experience option, visible within the Elevate progression rather than as a parallel track.Confirmed
2Begin tracking cross-enrollment data from Braven’s first Illinois Tech cohort to document the stacked-impact thesis with real numbers within 18–24 months.Fall 2026
3Prioritize the credit-bearing pathway for Elevate through Kaplan Institute and Stuart School conversations — this closes the most significant structural gap with Braven.Summer 2026
4Develop the working-professional coach layer for Elevate and integrate more corporate partnerships — sourcing volunteer coaches, sponsor employers, and Capstone partners from Sargent & Lundy, AWS, Kaplan, Fermilab, and new engagements.Fall 2026–Spring 2027
5Stand up the four-year mentorship roadmap formally — named milestones by year (FYE Y1, alumni matching Y2–Y3, industry mentor Y4) supported by Career-Services-owned mentorship infrastructure.By 2027

Athletics Partnership

Credentialing the leadership, discipline, and professionalism that varsity athletes, club-sport competitors, intramural participants, and cultural-sport players already demonstrate — built in collaboration with Illinois Tech Athletics.

Expansion expected · Summer 2026

The Elevate × Athletics expansion is scheduled to ship by Summer 2026.

Target: by the end of Summer 2026 every varsity, club, intramural, and cultural-sport participant has a clear Elevate badge pathway, and Career Services workshops are scheduled to be delivered to athletes by the team that grants their credentials. Watch this page for the formal Summer 2026 launch announcement.
